29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in $C$ and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a $k$-scheme $T$; the relative curve over $T$ is
37	$C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T$, with $\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C$ the
38	companion projection. Both $\pi_T$ and $\pi_C$ are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and $C/k$ is flat since $k$ is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ of the total space, and the
43	Picard group $\Pic(T)$ of the base. The pullback functor $\pi_T^*$ sends a line
44	bundle on $T$ to its base-change line bundle on $C \times_k T$; the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}[Line bundle on a product]
59	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
61	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
62	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
63	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
64	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
65	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
66	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
67	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
68	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$"
69	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
70	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
71	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
72	  Let $k$ be a base field, $C$ a $k$-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
73	  geometrically integral curve), and $T$ a $k$-scheme. A
74	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve $C \times_k T$} is an invertible
75	  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module, that is, an
76	  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module $\mathcal{L}$ such that there exists an
77	  open cover $\{U_i\}$ of $C \times_k T$ on which each restriction
78	  $\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}$ is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
79	  they are isomorphic as $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-modules. The set of
80	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
81	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
82	  $\Pic(C \times_k T)$. In Kleiman's notation with $X = C$ and $S = \Spec k$,
83	  this is exactly the value
84	  $\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)$ of the absolute Picard functor.
85	\end{definition}
86	
87	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
88	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
89	
90	If $f : Y \to Z$ is a morphism of schemes and $\mathcal{M}$ is an invertible
91	$\mathcal{O}_Z$-module, the pullback $f^*\mathcal{M}$ is an invertible
92	$\mathcal{O}_Y$-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising $\mathcal{M}$
93	trivialises $f^*\mathcal{M}$, and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
94	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
95	specialise it to the projection $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ and the
96	$\mathcal{O}_T$-Picard group.
97	
98	\begin{definition}[Pullback along the projection]
99	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
101	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
102	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
103	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
104	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
105	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
106	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
107	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
108	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
109	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
110	  %  [\dots]
111	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
112	  % sense: given any family of  $S$-schemes $T_i$, we have
113	  %     $$\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).$$"
114	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
115	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
116	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
117	  Let $C/k$ and $T/k$ be as in
118	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$
119	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along $\pi_T$} is the
120	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
121	  in this chapter)
122	  \[
123	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
124	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
125	  \]
126	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible $\mathcal{O}_T$-module
127	  $\mathcal{N}$ to the isomorphism class of $\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}$, an invertible
128	  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
129	  defined because pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules respects isomorphism, and is
130	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
131	  $\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C$ that we quotient by in the definition of
132	  the relative Picard presheaf.
133	\end{definition}
134	
135	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
136	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
139	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
141	  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
142	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
143	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
144	  % includes composition)
145	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
146	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
147	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
148	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
149	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
150	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$"
151	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
152	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
153	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
154	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
155	  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
156	  $g : T' \to T$ be a morphism of $k$-schemes. Write
157	  $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ and $\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'$ for the
158	  canonical projections, and write
159	  $g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$ for the
160	  base change of $g$ along $C/k$. Then on line bundles we have the equality of
161	  pullback maps
162	  \[
163	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
164	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
165	  \]
166	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
167	  \[
168	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
169	  \]
170	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
171	  $\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T$.
172	\end{lemma}
173	
174	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
175	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
176	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
177	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
178	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
179	\begin{proof}
180	  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
181	  The square
182	  \[
183	    \begin{array}{ccc}
184	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
185	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
186	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
187	    \end{array}
188	  \]
189	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
190	  composites $\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}$ holds on the nose. Pullback of
191	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules is functorial, so for any $\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)$
192	  there is a canonical isomorphism
193	  $g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}$
194	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
195	  $(\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}$
196	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying $\mathcal{O}$-module
197	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
198	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
199	  set-valued maps stated above.
200	\end{proof}
201	
202	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
203	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
204	
205	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
206	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
207	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
208	
209	\begin{theorem}[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
210	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
212	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection}
213	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
214	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
215	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
216	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
217	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
218	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
219	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
220	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
221	  % topologies  by
222	  %     $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
223	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
224	  %  \end{dfn}"
225	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
226	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
227	  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let $T$ be a
228	  $k$-scheme. The subset
229	  \[
230	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
231	  \]
232	  is the image of the pullback map $\pi_T^*$
233	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
234	  class and is closed under the multiplication of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ (so it is
235	  a subgroup). Define
236	  \[
237	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
238	  \]
239	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes $[\mathcal{L}]$ of
240	  line bundles $\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)$ under the relation
241	  $\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'$ iff
242	  $\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T$.
243	  Then $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a well-defined assignment from
244	  $k$-schemes to sets, and the natural map
245	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a surjection of sets.
246	\end{theorem}
247	
248	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
249	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
250	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
251	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
252	% not a translation of an external argument.
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
255	  The subset $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ is a subgroup of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$
256	  because the pullback map $\pi_T^*$ is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
257	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
258	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
259	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T$ is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
260	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
261	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
262	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is the canonical quotient map
263	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
264	\end{proof}
265	
266	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
267	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
268	
269	\begin{theorem}[Pullback descends through the quotient]
270	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
272	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection, lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
273	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
274	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
275	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
276	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
277	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
278	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
279	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
280	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
281	  %  [\dots]
282	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
283	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
284	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$"
285	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
286	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in $T$;
287	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
288	  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
289	  $g : T' \to T$ be a morphism of $k$-schemes. The pullback maps
290	  $g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')$ and
291	  $g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')$ make the diagram
292	  \[
293	    \begin{array}{ccc}
294	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
295	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
296	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
297	    \end{array}
298	  \]
299	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore $g_C^*$ sends
300	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ into $H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')$ and hence induces a
301	  unique set map
302	  \[
303	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
304	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
305	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
306	  \]
307	  through the quotients of
308	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
309	  $g \mapsto g^\sharp$ satisfies $\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ and
310	  $(g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp$ for any composable
311	  $h : T'' \to T'$ and $g : T' \to T$; thus
312	  $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ extends to a functor
313	  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$.
314	\end{theorem}
315	
316	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
317	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
318	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
319	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
320	\begin{proof}
321	  \uses{lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
322	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
323	  $g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*$ of
324	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore $g_C^*$ maps the image
325	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ into the image
326	  $H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')$: an element
327	  $\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T$ with $\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)$ has
328	  $g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}$. Hence
329	  $g_C^*$ induces a unique map of quotient sets
330	  $g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}$ by the
331	  universal property of the quotient set
332	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
333	  $\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ is immediate from
334	  $\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}$ for pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules. The
335	  composition law $(g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp$ follows from
336	  the corresponding statement for $\mathcal{O}$-module pullback
337	  $(g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*$ (passing through the quotient is functorial).
338	  Hence $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$ is a presheaf.
339	\end{proof}
340	
341	\section{Lean encoding}
342	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
343	
344	The Lean target file is the new
345	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
346	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
347	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
348	Lean targets as follows.
349	
350	\begin{itemize}
351	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
352	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
353	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on $C \times_k T$. Mathlib provides
354	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
355	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
356	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
357	    the product scheme.
358	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
359	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
360	    groups induced by $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$. The Mathlib backbone is
361	    the pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules
362	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
363	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
364	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
365	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
366	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
367	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
368	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
369	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
370	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
371	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
372	    structural existence of the quotient set
373	    $\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T$. Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
374	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
375	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
376	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
377	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
378	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
379	    $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$. The naturality square
380	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
381	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
382	    quotient-induced map.
383	\end{itemize}
384	
385	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
386	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
387	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
388	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
389	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of $C \times_k T$.

2043	\section{The Picard scheme}\label{sc:Psch}
2044	
2045	This section proves Grothendieck's main theorem about the Picard scheme,
2046	which asserts its existence if $X/S$ is projective and flat and its
2047	geometric fibers are integral; in fact, the functor $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ is
2048	representable.  The proof involves Grothendieck's method of using
2049	functors to prescribe patching.  The basic theory is developed in
2050	\cite[Ch.~0, Sct.~4.5, pp.~102--107]{EGAG}, and is applied in
2051	\cite[Ch.~1, Sct.~9, pp.~354--401]{EGAG} to the construction of
2052	Grassmannians and related parameter schemes.  The present construction
2053	involves the basic theory, but is more sophisticated and more
2054	complicated because it works not simply with the Zariski topology, but
2055	also with the \'etale topology.
2056	
2057	\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
2058	 If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
2059	is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
2060	scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
2061	Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists.
2062	 \end{dfn}
2063	
2064	Notice that, although there are four relative Picard functors, there is
2065	at most one Picard scheme.  Of course, if any functor is representable,
2066	then the representing scheme is uniquely determined, up to a unique
2067	isomorphism that preserves the identification of the given functor
2068	with the functor of points of the representing scheme.  But here, there
2069	is more to the story.
2070	
2071	Indeed, for example, say $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ is representable by
2072	$\IPic_{X/S}$.  Then, by descent theory, $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ is already a
2073	sheaf in the fppf topology; so it is equal to its associated sheaf
2074	$\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.  Hence, it too is representable by $\IPic_{X/S}$.
2075	On the other hand, $\Pic_{X/S}$ may or may not be representable;
2076	however, if it is, then it, as well, must be representable by $\IPic_{X/S}$.
2077	
2078	\begin{ex}\label{ex:0sec}
2079	 Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, and $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$
2080	holds universally.  Let $T$ be an $S$-scheme, and $\mc L$ an invertible
2081	sheaf on $X_T$.  Show that there exist a subscheme $N\subset T$ and an
2082	invertible sheaf $\mc N$ on $N$ with these three properties: first, $\mc
2083	L_N\simeq f_N^*{\mc N}$; second, given any $S$-map $t\:T'\to T$ such
2084	that $\mc L_{T'}\simeq f_{T'}^*{\mc N'}$ for some invertible sheaf $\mc
2085	N'$ on $T'$, necessarily $t$ factors through $N$ and $\mc N'\simeq
2086	t^*\mc N$; and third, $N$ is a closed subscheme if $\IPic_{X/S}$ is
2087	separated.  Show also that the first two properties determine $N$ uniquely
2088	and $\mc N$ up to isomorphism.
2089	 \end{ex}
2090	
2091	\begin{ex}\label{ex:univshf}
2092	 Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists.  An invertible sheaf $\mc P$ on
2093	$X\x\IPic_{X/S}$ is called a {\it universal sheaf}, or {\it Poincar\'e
2094	sheaf}, if $\mc P$ possesses the following property: given any
2095	$S$-scheme $T$ and any invertible sheaf $\mc L$ on $X_T$, there exists a
2096	unique $S$-map $h\:T\to\IPic_{X/S}$ such that, for some invertible sheaf
2097	$\mc N$ on $T$,
2098		$$\mc L\simeq (1\x h)^*\mc P\ox f_T^*\mc N.$$
2099	 Show that a universal sheaf $\mc P$ exists if and only if $\IPic_{X/S}$
2100	represents $\Pic_{X/S}$.
2101	
2102	Assume $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ holds universally.  Show that, if
2103	$\mc P$ exists, then it is unique up to tensor product with the pullback
2104	of a unique invertible sheaf on $\IPic_{X/S}$.
2105	
2106	Show that, if also $f$ has a section, then a universal sheaf $\mc P$
2107	exists.
2108	
2109	Find an example where no universal sheaf $\mc P$ exists.
2110	 \end{ex}
2111	
2112	\begin{ex}\label{ex:bschg}
2113	 Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, and let $S'$ be an $S$-scheme.
2114	Show that $\IPic_{X_{S'}/S'}$ exists too, and in fact, that
2115		$$\IPic_{X_{S'}/S'}=\IPic_{X/S}\x_SS'.$$
2116	%%  Thus the formation of the Picard scheme commutes with base change.
2117	 Thus forming the Picard scheme commutes with changing the base.
2118	
2119	Find an example where  $\IPic_{X_{S'}/S'}$ represents $\Pic_{X_{S'}/S'}$,
2120	but  $\IPic_{X/S}$ does not represent  $\Pic_{X/S}$.
2121	 \end{ex}
2122	
2123	\begin{ex}\label{ex:schpts}
2124	 Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, and either it represents
2125	$\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$ or $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ holds
2126	universally.  Show the scheme points of $\IPic_{X/S}$ correspond, in a
2127	natural bijective fashion, to the classes of invertible sheaves $\mc L$
2128	on the fibers of $X/S$.  A class is, by definition, represented by an
2129	$\mc L$ on an $X_k$ where $k$ is a field containing the residue field
2130	$k_s$ of a (scheme) point $s\in S$; an $\mc L'$ on an $X_{k'}$
2131	represents the same class if and only if there is a third field $k''$
2132	containing the other two such that $\mc L|X_{k''}\simeq\mc L'|X_{k''}$.
2133	 \end{ex}
2134	
2135	\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
2136	 The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
2137		$$A_{X/S}(T)\:\Div_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)$$
2138	 defined by sending a relative effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ to the
2139	sheaf $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)$.  The target $\Pic_{X/S}$ may be replaced by any
2140	of its associated sheaves.  %% And, of course,
2141	 If $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists,
2142	then the term {\it Abel map\/} may refer to the corresponding map of
2143	schemes
2144		$$\I A_{X/S}\:\IDiv_{X/S}\to\IPic_{X/S}.$$
2145	 \end{dfn}
2146	
2147	\begin{ex}\label{ex:PQ-Abel}
2148	  Assume $X/S$ is proper and flat with integral geometric fibers. Assume
2149	$\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, and denote it by $P$.  View $\IDiv_{X/S}$ as a
2150	$P$-scheme via the Abel map.  Assume a universal sheaf $\mc P$ exists,
2151	and let $\mc Q$ be the sheaf on $P$ associated to $\mc P$ as in
2152	Subsection~\ref{sb:Q}.  Show $\I P(\mc Q)=\IDiv_{X/S}$ as $P$-schemes.
2153	 \end{ex}
2154	
2155	\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
2156	 Assume $f\:X\to S$ is projective Zariski locally over $S$, and is flat
2157	with integral geometric fibers.
2158	
2159	\tu{(1)} Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, is separated and locally of finite
2160	type over $S$, and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.
2161	
2162	\tu{(2)} If also $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective, then
2163	$\IPic_{X/S}$ is a disjoint union of open
2164	 subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
2165	$S$-schemes.
2166	 \end{thm}
2167	
2168	\begin{proof}
2169	 By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on $S$ to
2170	represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of $S$-schemes.  Moreover, it
2171	is also a local matter on $S$ to prove that an $S$-scheme is separated
2172	and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
2173	$S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective.
2174	
2175	Plainly an $S$-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
2176	separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
2177	separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2).
2178	
2179	To prove (2), owing to Yoneda's lemma, we may view the category of
2180	schemes as a full subcategory of the category of functors by identifying
2181	a scheme $T$ with its functor of points.  Denote this functor too by $T$
2182	in order to lighten the notation. And say that the functor is a scheme,
2183	as well as that it is representable.  Also, set
2184	 $$P:=\Pic_{(X/S)\et}.$$
2185	  Note $P(T)= \Hom(T,P)$. %% \{\,T\to P\,\}$.
2186	
2187	Given a polynomial $\phi\in\bb Q[n]$, let $P^\phi\subset P$ be the
2188	\'etale subsheaf associated to the presheaf whose $T$-points are
2189	represented by the invertible sheaves $\mc L$ on $X_T$ such that we have
2190	 \begin{equation}\label{eq:phi}
2191		\chi(X_t,\mc L^{-1}_t(n)) = \phi(n) \text{ for all $t\in T$}.
2192	 \end{equation}
2193	 Notice that this presheaf is well defined, because (\ref{eq:phi})
2194	remains valid after any base change $p\:T'\to T$; indeed, for any $t'\in
2195	T'$, for any $i$, and for any $n$, we have
2196		$$\uH^i\bigl(X_{t'},\mc L^{-1}_{t'}(n)\bigr)
2197	   =\uH^i\bigl(X_{p(t')},\mc L^{-1}_{p(t')}(n)\bigr)\ox_{k_t}k_{t'}$$
2198	 because cohomology commutes with flat base change by \cite[Prp.~III,
2199	9.3, p.~255]{Ha83}.  Hence $P^\phi$ is well defined too.
2200	
2201	Fix a map $T\to P$, and represent it by means of an \'etale covering
2202	$p\:T'\to T$ and an invertible sheaf $\mc L'$ on $X_{T'}$.  Consider the
2203	subset ${T'}^{\phi}\subset T'$ defined as follows:
2204	       $${T'}^{\phi}:=\{\,t'\in T'\mid

2368	\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
2369	 Let $\alpha\:Z\to P$ be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set $R:=Z\x_PZ$.
2370	Assume $\alpha$ is a surjection, $Z$ is representable by a
2371	quasi-projective $S$-scheme, $R$ is representable by an $S$-scheme, and
2372	the first projection $R\to Z$ is representable by a smooth and proper
2373	map.  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $S$-scheme, and
2374	$\alpha$ is representable by a smooth map.
2375	 \end{lem}
2376	
2377	\begin{proof} To lighten the notation, let $Z$ and $R$ denote the
2378	corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map $Z\to S$ is
2379	quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
2380	$Z\x_SZ\to Z$ is too.  But the first projection $R\to Z$ is proper, and
2381	factors naturally through a map $h\:R\to Z\x_SZ$.  Hence $h$ is proper.
2382	But $h$ is a monomorphism; that is, $h$ is injective on $T$-points.
2383	Therefore, $h$ is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
2384	
2385	Plainly, for each $S$-scheme $T$, the subset $R(T)\subset
2386	Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
2387	map of schemes $R\to Z$ is flat and proper, and $Z$ is a
2388	quasi-projective $S$-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
2389	quasi-projective $S$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat and projective map
2390	$Z\to Q$ such that $R=Z\x_QZ$.  (In other words, a flat and proper
2391	equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)  In
2392	fact, $R$ defines a map from $Z$ to the Hilbert scheme $\IHilb_{Z/S}$, and
2393	its graph lies in the universal scheme as a closed subscheme, which
2394	descends to $Q\subset \IHilb_{Z/S}$; for more details, see \cite[Thm.~(2.9),
2395	p.~70]{AK80}.
2396	
2397	Since $Z\to Q$ is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
2398	smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
2399	same as those of $R\to Z$.  And $R\to Z$ is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
2400	$Z\to Q$ is smooth.
2401	
2402	It remains to see that $Q$ represents $P$.  First, observe that $Z\to Q$
2403	is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.  Indeed, given an
2404	element of $Q(T)$, set $A:=Z\x_QT$.  Then $A\to T$ is smooth.  So there
2405	exist an \'etale covering $T'\to T$ and a $T$-map $T'\to A$ by
2406	\cite[17.16.3 (ii)]{EGAIV4}.  Then $T'\to A\to Z$ is an element of
2407	$Z(T')$, which maps to the element of $Q(T')$ induced by the given
2408	element of $Q(T)$.
2409	
2410	Since $Z\to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, $Q$ is, in the
2411	category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
2412	$R\rightrightarrows Z$ by Exercise~\ref{ex:epi} below, which is an
2413	elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
2414	exercise, $P$ too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
2415	category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus $Q$
2416	represents $P$.
2417	 \end{proof}

2541	\cite[2.1.12]{EGAIII1}.
2542	
2543	Therefore, $\mc N$ is invertible at $t$, and forming $\mc N$ commutes
2544	with passing to $X'_t$, owing to the theory in Subsection~\ref{sb:Q}.
2545	So forming $u\:f_T^*\mc N\to \mc M$ commutes with passing to $X'_t$ too.
2546	But $u$ is an isomorphism on $X'_t$.  Hence $u$ is surjective along
2547	$X'_t$ by Nakayama's lemma. But both source and target of $u$ are
2548	invertible along $X'_t$.  Hence $u$ is an isomorphism along $X'_t$, so
2549	over $T_\sigma$ as $t\in T_\sigma$ is arbitrary.  Now, $g_T^*\mc
2550	M=\mc O_{\!T}$ and $g_T^*f_T^*=1$.  Hence $\mc N|T_\sigma= \mc
2551	O_{T_\sigma}$.  Therefore, $\mc L|X'_{T_\sigma}=(1\x\tau)^*\mc
2552	P'_{a,b}|X'_{T_\sigma}$.
2553	 
2554	Let $T_{a,b}'$ be the set of $t\in T$ such that $\tau(t)\in S'$ is the
2555	closed point and $\mc L|L_t$ has degree $a$ and $\mc L|M_t$ has degree
2556	$b$.  Fix $t\in T_{a,b}'$.  Then $\mc M|L_t$ has degree 0, so $\mc
2557	M|L_t\simeq \mc O_{L_t}$.  Similarly, $\mc M|M_t\simeq \mc O_{M_t}$.
2558	Consider the natural short exact sequence
2559		$$0\to\mc O_{L_t}(-1)\to\mc O_{\!X'_t}\to\mc O_{M_t}\to 0.$$
2560	 Twist  by $\mc M$ and take cohomology.  Thus $\uH^1(X'_t,\mc M_t)=0$ and
2561	$\uH^0(X'_t,\mc M_t)=k_t$.  Hence, on $X'_t$, the map $u$ becomes a map $\mc
2562	O_{\!X'_t}\to \mc M_t$.  This map is surjective as it is surjective after
2563	restriction to $L_t$ and to $M_t$.  So this map is an isomorphism.
2564	
2565	As above, we conclude $u$ is an isomorphism on an open neighborhood $V'$
2566	of $X'_t$.  Set $W':=f_{T'}(X'_T-V')$.  Since $f$ is proper, $W'$ is
2567	open.  But $f^{-1}W'\subset V'$.  So $u$ is an isomorphism over $W'$.
2568	Hence $O_{\!X'_{t'}}\risom \mc M_{t'}$ for all $t'\in W'$.  So $W'\subset
2569	T_{a,b}'\cup T_\sigma$.
2570	
2571	Set $T_{a,b}:=T_{a,b}'\cup T_\sigma$.  Then $T_{a,b}\subset T$ is open
2572	as it contains a neighborhood of each of its points.  Furthermore, $u$
2573	is an isomorphism over $T_{a,b}$.  Hence $\mc N|T_\sigma= \mc
2574	O_{T_\sigma}$, again since $g_T^*\mc M=\mc O_{\!T}$ and $g_T^*f_T^*=1$.
2575	Therefore, $\mc L|X'_{T_{a,b}}=(1\x\tau)^*\mc P'_{a,b}|X'_{T_{a,b}}$.
2576

2578	structure map $\tau\:T\to S'$ and the inclusion of $S'$ in $S'_n$ as
2579	$S'_{a,b}$.  Plainly $(1\x q_{a,b})^*\mc P'=\mc L|X'_{T_{a,b}}$.
2580	Plainly, as $a$ and $b$ and $n$ vary, the $q_{a,b}$ patch to a map
2581	$q\:T\to\bb \amalg S'_n$ such that $(1\x q)^*\mc P'= \mc L$.  Plainly
2582	this map $q$ is the only $S'$-map $q$ such that $(1\x q)^*\mc P'\simeq
2583	\mc L\ox f_T^*\mc N$ for some invertible sheaf $\mc N$ on $T$.  Thus

2600	\begin{prp}\label{prp:lft}
2601	 If $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$, then
2602	$\IPic_{X/S}$ is locally of finite type.
2603	 \end{prp}
2604	 \begin{proof}
2605	 Set $P:=\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.  Owing to \cite[8.14.2]{EGAIV3}, we just
2606	need to check the following condition.  For every filtered inverse system
2607	of affine $S$-schemes $T_i$, the natural map is a bijection:
2608	 \begin{equation*}\label{equation:lft}
2609	 \varinjlim P(T_i) \risom P\bigl(\varprojlim T_i\bigr).
2610	 \end{equation*}
2611	
2612	To check it is injective, set $T:=\varprojlim T_i$.  Fix $i$ and let
2613	$\lambda_i\in P(T_i)$.  Represent $\lambda_i$ by a sheaf $\mc L_i'$ on
2614	$X_{T_i'}$ where $T_i'\to T_i$ is an fppf covering.  Set
2615	$T':=T_i'\x_{T_i}T$.  Set $\mc L':=\mc L_i|X_{T'}$.  Let $\lambda$ be
2616	the image of $\lambda_i$ in $P(T)$.  Then $\mc L'$ represents $\lambda$.
2617	  
2618	Suppose $\lambda=0\in P(T)$.  Then there exists an fppf covering $T''\to
2619	T'$ such that $\mc L'|X_{T''}\simeq \mc O_{\!X_{T''}}$.  It follows from
2620	\cite[8.8.2, 8.10.5(vi), 11.2.6, 8.5.2(i), and 8.5.2.4]{EGAIV3} that
2621	there exist a $j\ge i$ and an fppf covering $T_j''\to T_j'$ with
2622	$T_j':=T_i'\x_{T_j}T$ such that $\mc L'|X_{T_j''}\simeq \mc
2623	O_{\!X_{T_j''}}$.  So  $\lambda_i$ maps to $0\in P(T_j)$.  Thus the map
2624	is injective. 
2625	
2626	Surjectivity can be proved similarly. \end{proof}
2627	
2628	\begin{rmk}\label{rk:exist}
2629	 There are three important existence theorems for $\IPic_{X/S}$, which
2630	refine Theorem~\ref{th:main}.  They were proved soon after it, and each
2631	involves new ideas.
2632	 
2633	First, Mumford proved the following generalization of
2634	Theorem~\ref{th:main}, and it fits in nicely with
2635	Example~\ref{eg:Mumford}.
2636	\begin{sbsthm}\label{th:Mumford}%\hangindent\parindent
2637	 Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat, and its geometric fibers are
2638	reduced and connected; assume the irreducible components of its ordinary
2639	fibers are  geometrically irreducible.  Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists.
2640	 \end{sbsthm}
2641	
2642	Mumford stated this theorem at the bottom of Page viii in \cite{Mm66}.
2643	He said the proof is a generalization of that \cite[Lects.~19--21]{Mm66}
2644	in the case where $S$ is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field
2645	and $X$ is a smooth surface.  That proof is based on his theory of
2646	independent 0-cycles.  This theory is further developed in
2647	\cite[pp.~23--28]{AK79} and used to prove \cite[Thm.~(3.1)]{AK79}, which
2648	asserts the existence of a natural compactification of $\IPic_{X/S}$
2649	when $X/S$ is flat and locally projective with integral geometric
2650	fibers.
2651	
2652	On the other hand, Grothendieck \cite[p.~236-1]{FGA} attributed to
2653	Mumford a slightly different theorem, in which neither the geometric
2654	fibers nor the ordinary fibers are assumed connected (see
2655	\cite[p.~210]{BLR} also).  Grothendieck said the proof is based on a
2656	refinement of Mumford's construction of quotients, and referred to the
2657	forthcoming notes of a Harvard seminar of Mumford and Tate's, held in
2658	the spring of 1962.
2659	
2660	Mumford was kind enough, in November 2003, to mail the present author
2661	his personal folder of handwritten notes from the seminar; the folder is
2662	labeled, ``Groth--Mumford--Tate,'' and contains notes from talks by each
2663	of the three, and notes written by each of them.  Virtually all the content
2664	has appeared elsewhere; Mumford's contributions appeared in Mumford's
2665	books \cite{Mm65}, \cite{Mm66}, and \cite{Mm70}.
2666	
2667	The notes contain, in Mumford's hand, a precise statement of the theorem
2668	and a rough sketch of the proof.  This statement too is slightly
2669	different from that of Theorem ~\ref{th:Mumford}: he crossed out the
2670	hypothesis that the geometric fibers are connected; and he made the
2671	weaker assumption that the ordinary fibers are connected.  The proof is
2672	broadly like his proof in \cite[Lects.~19--21]{Mm66}.
2673	 
2674	\smallskip
2675	Second, Grothendieck proved this theorem of ``generic
2676	representability.''
2677	  \begin{sbsthm}\label{th:genrep}
2678	 Assume $X/S$ is proper, and $S$ is integral.  Then there exists a
2679	nonempty open subset $V\subset S$ such that $\IPic_{X_V/V}$ exists,
2680	represents $\Pic_{(X_V/V)\fppf}$, and is a disjoint union of open
2681	quasi-projective subschemes.
2682	 \end{sbsthm}
2683	
2684	 A particularly important special case is covered by the
2685	following corollary.
2686	  \begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
2687	  Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$, and $X$ is complete.  Then
2688	$\IPic_{X/k}$ exists, represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$, and is a disjoint
2689	union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
2690	 \end{sbscor}
2691	
2692	Before Grothendieck discovered Theorem~\ref{th:genrep}, he
2693	\cite[Cor.~6.6, p.~232-17]{FGA} proved Corollary~\ref{cor:algsch}
2694	assuming $X/k$ is projective.  To do so, he developed a method of
2695	``relative representability,'' by which Theorem~\ref{th:main} implies
2696	the existence of the Picard scheme in other cases.  The method
2697	incorporates a ``d\'evissage'' of Oort's \cite[\S 6]{Oo62}; the latter
2698	yields the Picard scheme as an extension of a group subscheme of
2699	$\IPic_{X_{\red}/k}$ by an affine group scheme.
2700	
2701	In \cite[Rem.~6.6, p.~232-17]{FGA}, Grothendieck said it is ``extremely
2702	plausible'' that Corollary~\ref{cor:algsch} holds in general, and can be
2703	proved by extending the method of relative representability, so that it
2704	covers the case of a surjective map $X'\to X$ with $X'$ projective, such
2705	as the map provided by Chow's lemma \cite[Thm.~5.6.1]{EGAII}.
2706	Furthermore, he conjectured, in \cite[Rem.~6.6, p.~232-17]{FGA}, that,
2707	for any surjective map $X'\to X$ between proper schemes over a field,
2708	the induced map on Picard schemes is affine.  He said he was led to the
2709	conjecture by considerations of ``nonflat descent,'' a version of
2710	descent theory where the maps are not required to be flat, but the
2711	objects are.
2712	
2713	Thanking Grothendieck for help, Murre \cite[(II.15)]{Mr63} gave the
2714	first proof of the heart of Corollary~\ref{cor:algsch}: if $X/k$ is
2715	proper, then $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and
2716	is locally of finite type.  Murre did not use the method of relative
2717	representability.  Rather, he identified seven conditions
2718	\cite[(I.2.1)]{Mr63} that are necessary and sufficient for the
2719	representability of a functor from schemes over a field to Abelian
2720	groups.  Then he showed the seven are satisfied by the Picard functor
2721	localized in the fpqc topology.  To handle the last two conditions, he
2722	used Chow's lemma and Theorem~\ref{th:main}.
2723	
2724	In the meantime, Grothendieck had proved Theorem~\ref{th:genrep},
2725	according to Murre \cite[p.~5]{Mr63}.  Later, the proof appeared in two
2726	parts.  The first part established a key intermediate result, the
2727	following theorem.
2728	 \begin{sbsthm}\label{th:flatten}
2729	  Assume $X/S$ is proper.  Let $\mc F$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$, and
2730	$S_{\mc F}\subset S$ the subfunctor of all $S$-schemes $T$ such that
2731	${\mc F}_T$ is $T$-flat.  Then $S_{\mc F}$ is representable by an
2732	unramified $S$-scheme of finite type.
2733	 \end{sbsthm}
2734	 %%\noindent 
2735	 Murre sketched Grothendieck's proof of this theorem in \cite[Cor.~1,
2736	   p.~294-11]{Mr65}.  The proof involves identifying and checking eight
2737	 conditions that are necessary and sufficient for the representability
2738	 of a functor by a separated and unramified $S$-scheme locally of finite
2739	 type.  As in Murre's proof of Corollary~\ref{cor:algsch}, a key step is
2740	 to show the functor is ``pro-representable'': there exist certain
2741	 natural topological rings, and if the functor is representable by $Y$,
2742	 then these rings are the completions of the local rings at the points

2154	\section{Construction of Quot Schemes}
2155	
2156	\medskip
2157	
2158	
2159	
2160	\centerline{\large\bf Notions of Projectivity}
2161	
2162	\medskip
2163	
2164	
2165	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme. Recall that as defined by Grothendieck, 
2166	a morphism $X\to S$ is called a \textbf{projective morphism}
2167	if there exists a coherent sheaf $E$ on $S$, together with a 
2168	closed embedding of $X$ into $\PP(E) = \Proj \sym_{{\mathcal O}_S} E $ 
2169	over $S$. Equivalently, $X\to S$ is projective when 
2170	it is proper and there exists a relatively very ample
2171	line bundle $L$ on $X$ over $S$. These conditions are related by
2172	taking $L$ to be the restriction of ${\mathcal O}_{\PP(E)}(1)$ to $X$, or
2173	in the reverse direction, taking $E$ to be the direct image of $L$ on $S$.
2174	A morphism $X\to S$ is called \textbf{quasi-projective} if it factors as 
2175	an open embedding $X\hra Y$ followed by a projective morphism $Y \to S$.
2176	
2177	
2178	A stronger version of projectivity was introduced 
2179	by Altman and Kleiman: a morphism
2180	$X\to S$ of noetherian schemes is called \textbf{strongly projective} 
2181	(respectively, \textbf{strongly quasi-projective}) if
2182	there exists a vector bundle $E$ on $S$ together with a closed
2183	embedding (respectively, a locally closed embedding)
2184	$X\subset \PP(E)$ over $S$. 
2185	
2186	
2187	Finally, the strongest version of (quasi-)projectivity 
2188	is as follows (used for example in
2189	the textbook [H] by Hartshorne): a morphism
2190	$X\to S$ of noetherian schemes is projective in the strongest sense 
2191	if $X$ admits a (locally-)closed embedding into $\PP^n_S$ for some $n$.
2192	
2193	Note that none of the three versions of projectivity
2194	is local over the base $S$.
2195	
2196	\medskip
2197	
2198	\textbf{Exercises } (i) Gives examples to show that the above
2199	three notions of projectivity are in general distinct. 
2200	
2201	(ii) Show that if $X\to S$ is projective and flat, where $S$ is noetherian,
2202	then $X\to S$ is strongly projective.
2203	
2204	(iii) Note that if every coherent sheaf of ${\mathcal O}_S$-modules
2205	is the quotient of a vector bundle, then projectivity over the base $S$ 
2206	is equivalent to strong projectivity. If $S$ admits an ample line bundle 
2207	(for example, if $S$ is quasi-projective over an affine base), 
2208	then all three notions of projectivity over $S$ 
2209	are equivalent to each other. 
2210	
2211	
2212	
2213	
2214	
2215	
2216	\bigskip
2217	
2218	\newpage
2219	
2220	\centerline{\large\bf Main Existence Theorems}
2221	
2222	
2223	\medskip
2224	
2225	
2226	
2227	Grothendieck's original theorem on Quot schemes, 
2228	whose proof is outlined in [FGA] TDTE-IV, is the following.
2229	
2230	\begin{theorem}\label{Grothendieck Quot} {\rm (Grothendieck)}  
2231	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi: X\to S$ a projective morphism,
2232	and $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$. Then for any 
2233	coherent ${\mathcal O}_X$-module $E$ and any polynomial $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$,
2234	the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2235	by a projective $S$-scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$.
2236	\end{theorem}
2237	
2238	
2239	Altman and Kleiman gave a complete and detailed proof of the existence of
2240	Quot schemes in [A-K 2]. They could remove the noetherian hypothesis, 
2241	by instead assuming strong (quasi-)projectivity of $X\to S$ 
2242	together with an assumption about the nature of the coherent  
2243	sheaf $E$, and deduce that the scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is then
2244	strongly (quasi-)projective over $S$.
2245	
2246	For simplicity, in these lecture notes we state and prove the result in [A-K 2]
2247	in the noetherian context.
2248	
2249	\begin{theorem}\label{Altman-Kleiman Quot} {\rm (Altman-Kleiman)}
2250	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $X$ a closed subscheme of 
2251	$\PP(V)$ for some vector bundle $V$ on $S$, $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)|_X$,
2252	$E$ a coherent quotient sheaf of $\pi^*(W)(\nu)$ where
2253	$W$ is a vector bundle on $S$ and $\nu$ is an integer, and 
2254	$\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$. 
2255	Then the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2256	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which can be embedded over $S$ as a 
2257	closed subscheme of $\PP(F)$ for some vector bundle $F$ on $S$.
2258	
2259	The vector bundle $F$ can be chosen to be an exterior power of 
2260	the tensor product of $W$ with a symmetric powers of $V$.
2261	\end{theorem}
2262	
2263	Taking both $V$ and $W$ to be trivial in the above, we get the following.
2264	
2265	\begin{theorem}
2266	If $S$ is a noetherian scheme, $X$ is a closed subscheme of 
2267	$\PP^n_S$ for some $n\ge 0$, $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}(1)|_X$,
2268	$E$ is a coherent quotient sheaf of $\oplus^p {\mathcal O}_X(\nu)$ 
2269	for some integers $p\ge 0$ and $\nu$, and $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$,
2270	then the the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2271	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which can be embedded over $S$ as a 
2272	closed subscheme of $\PP^r_S$ for some $r\ge 0$.
2273	\end{theorem}
2274	
2275	
2276	The rest of this section is devoted to proving 
2277	Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot}, with extra noetherian hypothesis.
2278	At the end, we will remark on how the proof also
2279	gives us the original version of Grothendieck.
2280	
2281	
2282	
2283	
2284	
2285	
2286	\bigskip
2287	
2288	\centerline{\large\bf Reduction to the case of 
2289	$\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$}
2290	
2291	\medskip
2292	
2293	
2294	It is enough to prove Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot} in the special case
2295	that $X = \PP(V)$ and
2296	$E= \pi^*(W)$ where $V$ and $W$ are vector bundles on $S$, as
2297	a consequence of the next lemma.
2298	
2299	\begin{lemma}
2300	(i) Let $\nu$ be any integer. 
2301	Then tensoring by $L^{\nu}$ gives an isomorphism of
2302	functors from $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ to 
2303	$\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}$ where the polynomial 
2304	$\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]$ is defined by $\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$. 
2305	
2306	(ii) Let $\phi : E \to G$ be a surjective homomorphism of 
2307	coherent sheaves on $X$. Then the corresponding natural transformation
2308	$\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$
2309	is a closed embedding.
2310	\end{lemma}
2311	
2312	\proof The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just says that
2313	given any locally noetherian scheme $T$ and a family
2314	$\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)$, there exists
2315	a closed subscheme $T' \subset T$ with the following universal property:
2316	for any locally noetherian scheme $U$ and a morphism $f: U\to T$,
2317	the pulled back homomorphism of ${\mathcal O}_{X_U}$-modules $q_U : E_U \to \F_U$
2318	factors via the pulled back homomorphism $\phi_U : E_U \to G_U$
2319	if and only if $U \to T$ factors via $T'\hra T$. 
2320	This is satisfied by taking $T'$ to be the vanishing scheme
2321	for the composite homomorphism $\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F$
2322	of coherent sheaves on $X_T$ (see Remark \ref{vanishing scheme}), 
2323	which makes sense here as both $\ker(\phi)$ and $\F$ are coherent 
2324	on $X_T$ and $\F$ is flat over $T$. \qed
2325	
2326	
2327	
2328	Therefore if $\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is
2329	representable, then for any coherent quotient
2330	$E$ of $\pi^*W(\nu)|_X$, we can take $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ 
2331	to be a closed subscheme of $\quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$.
2332	
2333	
2334	
2335	
2336	
2337	
2338	
2339	
2340	
2341	
2342	
2343	
2344	\bigskip
2345	
2346	\centerline{\large\bf Use of $m$-Regularity}
2347	
2348	\medskip
2349	
2350	We consider the sheaf $E = \pi^*(W)$ on $X=\PP(V)$ where
2351	$V$ is a vector bundle on $S$,
2352	and take $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)$. 
2353	For any field $k$ and a $k$-valued point 
2354	$s$ of $S$, we have an isomorphism 
2355	$\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k$ where $n=\rank(V)-1$,
2356	and the restricted sheaf $E_s$ on $\PP(V)_s$ is isomorphic to 
2357	$\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}$ where $p=\rank(W)$. 
2358	It follows from Theorem \ref{Mumford}
2359	that given any $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$, there exists an integer
2360	$m$ which depends only on $\rank(V)$, $\rank(W)$ and $\Phi$,
2361	such that for any field $k$ and a $k$-valued point $s$ of $S$,
2362	the sheaf $E_s$ on $\PP(V)_s$ is $m$-regular, and for any  
2363	coherent quotient $q: E_s \to \F$ on $\PP(V)_s$ with 
2364	Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$, the sheaf $\F$ and the kernel sheaf
2365	$\G\subset E_s$ of $q$ are both $m$-regular. 
2366	In particular, it follows from the Castelnuovo Lemma \ref{Castelnuovo}
2367	that for $r\ge m$, all cohomologies 
2368	$H^i(X_s,E_s(r))$, $H^i(X_s,\F(r))$, and $H^i(X_s,\G(r))$ 
2369	are zero for $i\ge 1$,
2370	and $H^0(X_s,E_s(r))$, $H^0(X_s,\F(r))$, and $H^0(X_s,\G(r))$ 
2371	are generated by their global sections.
2372	
2373	From the above it follows by Theorem \ref{Base-change for flat sheaves}
2374	that if $T$ is an $S$-scheme and
2375	$q :E_T \to \F$ is a $T$-flat coherent quotient with Hilbert polynomial
2376	$\Phi$, then we have the following, where $\G\subset E_T$ is
2377	the kernel of $q$.
2378	
2379	\textbf{(*) } The sheaves ${\pi_T}_*\G(r)$, ${\pi_T}_*E_T(r)$, 
2380	${\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ are locally free of fixed ranks 
2381	determined by the data $n$, $p$, $r$, and $\Phi$, 
2382	the homomorphisms ${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to \G(r)$, 
2383	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(E_T(r))\to E_T(r)$, 
2384	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\F(r))\to \F(r)$ are 
2385	surjective, and the higher direct images  
2386	$R^i{\pi_T}_*\G(r)$, $R^i{\pi_T}_*E_T(r)$, 
2387	$R^i{\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ are zero, for all $r\ge m$ and $i\ge 1$. 
2388	
2389	\textbf{(**) }
2390	In particular we have the following commutative diagram
2391	of locally sheaves on $X_T$, in which both rows are exact, and all 
2392	three vertical maps are surjective.
2393	
2394	%{\footnotesize
2395	$$\begin{array}{ccccccc}
2396	0  \to & {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r)) & \to 
2397	& {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_* (E_T(r)) &
2398	\to  &{\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_* (\F(r))& \to   0\\ 
2399	       & \dna                &     & \dna                &
2400	     & \dna               &        \\
2401	0  \to & \G(r)                  & \to & E(r) &
2402	\to  & \F(r)                 & \to   0
2403	\end{array}$$
2404	%}% end of footnotesize
2405	
2406	\bigskip
2407	
2408	
2409	\centerline{\large\bf Embedding Quot into Grassmannian}
2410	
2411	\medskip
2412	
2413	We now fix a positive integer $r$ such that $r\ge m$.
2414	Note that the rank of ${\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ is $\Phi(r)$
2415	and $\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V$.
2416	Therefore the surjective homomorphism
2417	${\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$
2418	defines an element of the set 
2419	$\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)$.
2420	We thus get a morphism of functors 
2421	$$\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2422	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$$
2423	It associates to $q: E_T \to \F$ the quotient
2424	${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$.
2425	 
2426	The above morphism $\alpha$ is injective because the quotient 
2427	$q: E_T \to \F$ can be recovered from 
2428	${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ 
2429	as follows. 
2430	
2431	If $G = \grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ with
2432	projection $p_G : G \to S$, and
2433	$u : {p_G}^*E \to \U$ denotes the universal quotient on $G$
2434	with kernel $v : \K \to {p_G}^*E$,
2435	then the homomorphism 
2436	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*E_T(r)$ 
2437	can be recovered 
2438	from the morphism $T \to G$ as
2439	the pull-back of $v : \K \to {p_G}^*E$.
2440	Let $h$ be  the composite 
2441	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(E_T(r))\to E_T(r)$.
2442	As a consequence of the properties of the diagram \textbf{(**)}, 
2443	the following is a right exact sequence on $X_T$
2444	$${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\stackrel{h}{\to} 
2445	E_T(r) \stackrel{q(r)}{\to} \F \to 0$$
2446	and so $q(r) : E_T(r) \to \F(r)$ can be recovered as the cokernel of $h$.
2447	Finally, twisting by $-r$, we recover $q$, proving the desired 
2448	injectivity of the morphism of functors 
2449	$\alpha :\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2450	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$.
2451	
2452	
2453	
2454	\bigskip
2455	
2456	\centerline{\large\bf Use of Flattening Stratification}
2457	\nopagebreak
2458	\medskip
2459	\nopagebreak
2460	We will next prove that 
2461	$\alpha :\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2462	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$
2463	is relatively representable. In fact, we will show that
2464	given any locally noetherian $S$-scheme $T$ and
2465	a surjective homomorphism $f :  W_T \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_T}\sym^r V_T \to \J$
2466	where $\J$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module of rank $\Phi(r)$,
2467	there exists a locally closed subscheme $T'$ of 
2468	$T$ with the following universal property \textbf{(F)} : 
2469	
2470	\textbf{(F) } Given any 
2471	locally noetherian $S$-scheme $Y$ and an $S$-morphism $\phi :Y\to T$,
2472	let $f_Y$ be the pull-back of $f$, and  
2473	let $\K_Y = \ker(f_Y) = \phi^* \ker(f)$. Let
2474	$\pi_Y : X_Y \to Y$ be the projection, and let
2475	$h : {\pi_Y}^* \K_Y \to E_Y$ be the composite map
2476	$${\pi_Y}^* \K_Y \to  {\pi_Y}^* (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V)
2477	= {\pi_Y}^*{\pi_Y}_*E_Y \to  E_Y$$ 
2478	Let $q:   E_Y \to \F$ be the cokernel of $h$. 
2479	Then $\F$ is flat over $Y$ with its Hilbert polynomial on all fibers
2480	equal to $\Phi$ if and only if $\phi :Y\to T$
2481	factors via $Y'\hra Y$.
2482	
2483	The existence of such a locally closed subscheme $T'$ of 
2484	$T$ is given by Theorem \ref{flattening stratification}, which 
2485	shows that $T'$ is the stratum corresponding to 
2486	Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$ for the 
2487	flattening stratification over $T$ for the sheaf $\F$ on $X_T$.
2488	
2489	When we take $T$ to be $\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ 
2490	with universal quotient $u : {p_G}^*E \to \U$, the corresponding
2491	locally closed subscheme $T'$ represents
2492	the functor $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ by its construction.
2493	
2494	Hence we have shown that $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ is represented
2495	by a locally closed subscheme of 
2496	$\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$. As 
2497	$\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ embeds as a 
2498	closed subscheme of $\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V)$,
2499	we get a locally closed embedding of $S$-schemes 
2500	$$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \subset 
2501	\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V))$$
2502	In particular, the morphism $\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ 
2503	is separated and of finite type.
2504	
2505	
2506	
2507	\bigskip
2508	
2509	
2510	\centerline{\large\bf Valuative Criterion for Properness}
2511	
2512	
2513	\medskip
2514	
2515	The original reference for the following argument is EGA IV (2) 2.8.1. 
2516	
2517	The functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ satisfies the following valuative
2518	criterion for properness over $S$: given any discrete valuation ring
2519	$R$ over $S$ with quotient field $K$, the restriction map
2520	$$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$$
2521	is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
2522	Given any coherent quotient 
2523	$q: E_K \to \F$ on $X_R$ which defines an element $\langle \F, q\rangle$
2524	of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$.
2525	Let $\ov{\F}$ be the image of the composite homomorphism
2526	$E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F$ where $j : X_K \hra X_R$ is the open
2527	inclusion. Let $\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}$ be the induced surjection.
2528	Then we leave it to the reader to verify that 
2529	$\langle \F, q\rangle$ is an element of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)$
2530	which maps to $\langle \F, q\rangle$, and is the unique such element.
2531	(Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr 
2532	is the same as being torsion-free.)
2533	
2534	As $S$ is noetherian and as we have already shown that 
2535	$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ is of finite type,
2536	it follows that $\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ is a proper morphism.
2537	Therefore the embedding of
2538	$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ into 
2539	$\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V))$
2540	is a closed embedding.
2541	
2542	This completes the proof of Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot}.\hfill$\square$
2543	
2544	
2545	
2546	
2547	
2548	\bigskip
2549	
2550	
2551	\centerline{\large\bf The Version of Grothendieck}
2552	
2553	
2554	\medskip
2555	
2556	
2557	We now describe how to get Theorem \ref{Grothendieck Quot}
2558	from the above proof. As $S$ is noetherian, we can find a common
2559	$m$ such that given any field-valued point 
2560	$s: \spec k\to S$ and a coherent quotient $q: E_s \to \F$ on $X_s$
2561	with Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$,
2562	the sheaves $E_s(r)$, $\F(r)$, $\G(r)$ (where $\G = \ker(q)$) are
2563	generated by global sections and all their higher cohomologies vanish,
2564	whenever $r\ge m$.
2565	This follows from the theory of $m$-regularity, and semi-continuity. 
2566	
2567	Because we have such a common $m$, we get as before an injective morphism
2568	from the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ into the Grassmannian functor
2569	$\Grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$. The sheaf 
2570	$\pi_*E(r)$ is coherent, but need not be the quotient of a 
2571	vector bundle on $S$. Consequently, the scheme $\grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$
2572	is projective over the base, but not necessarily strongly projective.
2573	
2574	Finally, the use of flattening stratification, which can be 
2575	made over an affine open cover of $S$, gives 
2576	a locally closed subscheme of $\grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$ which represents
2577	$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$, which is in fact a
2578	closed subscheme by the valuative criterion. 
2579	Thus, we get $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ as a projective scheme over $S$.
2580

2604	\section{Some Variants and Applications}
2605	
2606	\medskip
2607	
2608	\centerline{\large\bf Quot Scheme in Quasi-Projective case}
2609	
2610	
2611	\medskip
2612	
2613	
2614	
2615	\textbf{Exercise } 
2616	Let $\pi : Z\to S$ be a proper morphism of noetherian schemes. 
2617	Let $Y\subset Z$ be a closed subscheme, and let $\F$ be a coherent sheaf
2618	on $Z$. Then there exists an open subscheme $S' \subset S$ 
2619	with the universal property that a morphism $T\to S$ factors through
2620	$S'$ if and only if the support of the pull-back
2621	$\F_T$ on $Z_T = Z\times_ST$ is disjoint from
2622	$Y_T = Y \times_ST$.
2623	
2624	
2625	
2626	\medskip
2627	
2628	
2629	
2630	\textbf{Exercise } As a consequence of the above, show the following:  
2631	If $\pi : Z\to S$ is a proper morphism with $S$ noetherian, if
2632	$X\subset Z$ is an open subscheme, and if
2633	$E$ is a coherent sheaf on $Z$, then 
2634	$\Quot_{E|_X/X/S}$ is an open subfunctor of $\Quot_{E/Z/S}$. 
2635	
2636	
2637	
2638	\medskip
2639	
2640	
2641	With the above preparation, the construction of 
2642	a quot scheme extends to the strongly quasi-projective case, to
2643	give the following.
2644	
2645	\begin{theorem}\label{Strongly quasi-projective quot}
2646	{\rm (Altman and Kleiman)} 
2647	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $X$ a locally closed subscheme of 
2648	$\PP(V)$ for some vector bundle $V$ on $S$, $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)|_X$,
2649	$E$ a coherent quotient sheaf of $\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X$ where
2650	$W$ is a vector bundle on $S$ and $\nu$ is an integer, and 
2651	$\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$. 
2652	Then the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2653	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which can be embedded over $S$ as a

2654	locally closed subscheme of $\PP(F)$ for some vector bundle $F$ on $S$.
2655	Moreover, the vector bundle $F$ can be chosen to be an exterior power of 
2656	the tensor product of $W$ with a symmetric power of $V$.
2657	\end{theorem}
2658	
2659	\proof Let $\ov{X} \subset \PP(V)$ be the schematic closure of 
2660	$X\subset \PP(V)$, and let $\ov{E}$ be the coherent sheaf on $\ov{X}$
2661	defined as the image of the composite homomorphism
2662	$$\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_{\ov{X}} \to j_*(\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X) \to j_*E$$
2663	Then we get a quotient $\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_{\ov{X}} \to \ov{E}$
2664	which restricts on $X\subset \ov{X}$ to the given quotient
2665	$\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X\to E$. Therefore by the above exercise,
2666	$\Quot_{E/X/S}$ is an open subfunctor of 
2667	$\Quot_{\ov{E}/\ov{X}/S}$. Now the result follows from the 
2668	Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot}.\qed
2669	
2670	
2671	
2672	\medskip
2673	
2674	In order to extend Grothendieck's construction of a quot
2675	scheme to the quasi-projective case, one first needs the following lemma
2676	which is of independent interest.
2677	
2678	
2679	\begin{lemma}\label{coherent prolongation}
2680	Any coherent sheaf on an open subscheme of a noetherian scheme $S$ can be
2681	prolonged to a coherent sheaf on all of $S$.
2682	\end{lemma}
2683	
2684	\proof First consider the case where $S = \spec A$ is affine, and
2685	let $j: U\hra S$ denote the inclusion.
2686	The quasi-coherent sheaf 
2687	$j_*(\F)$ corresponds to the $A$-module $M = H^0(S,j_*(\F))$,
2688	in the sense that $j_*(\F) = M^{\sim}$. 
2689	Given any $u\in U$, there exist finitely many 
2690	elements $e_1,\ldots,e_n \in M$
2691	which generate the fiber $\F_u$ regarded as a vector space
2692	over the residue field $\kappa(u)$. By Nakayama these elements 
2693	will generate the stalks of $\F$ in an open neighbourhood of $u$ in $U$.
2694	Therefore by the noetherian hypothesis, there exist finitely many
2695	elements $e_1,\ldots,e_r \in M$ which generate the stalk of $\F$ 
2696	at each point of $U$. If $N\subset M$ is the submodule generated by these 
2697	elements, then $\G = N^{\sim}$ is a coherent prolongation of
2698	$\F$ to $S = \spec A$, proving the result in the affine case.
2699	
2700	In the general case, by the 
2701	noetherian condition there exists a maximal coherent prolongation
2702	$(U',\F')$ of $\F$. Then unless $U' = S$, we can obtain  
2703	a further prolongation of $\F'$ by using the affine case. 
2704	For, if $u\in S- U'$,
2705	we can take an affine open subscheme $V$ containing $u$, and a 
2706	coherent prolongation $\G'$ of $\F'|_{U'\bigcap V}$ to all of $V$,
2707	and then glue together $\G'$ and $\F'$ along $U'\bigcap V$ to 
2708	further prolong $\F'$ to $U'\bigcup V$, 
2709	contradicting the maximality of $(U',\F')$. 
2710	\qed
2711	
2712	
2713	\begin{theorem}\label{Quasi-projective quot}{\rm (Grothendieck)}
2714	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $X$ a quasi-projective 
2715	scheme over $S$, $L$ a line bundle on $X$ which is
2716	relatively very ample over $S$, $E$ a quotient sheaf on $X$, 
2717	and $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$. 
2718	Then the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2719	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which is
2720	quasi-projective over $S$.
2721	\end{theorem}
2722	
2723	\proof By definition of quasi-projectivity of $X\to S$, note that
2724	$X$ can be embedded over $S$ as a locally closed subscheme of 
2725	$\PP(V)$ for some coherent sheaf $V$ on $S$, 
2726	such that $L$ is isomorphic to ${\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)|_X$.
2727	Let $\ov{X} \subset \PP(V)$ be the schematic closure of
2728	$X$ in $\PP(V)$. This is a projective scheme over $S$, and 
2729	$X$ is embedded as an open subscheme in it. 
2730	By Lemma \ref{coherent prolongation} the
2731	coherent sheaf $E$ has a coherent prolongation $\ov{E}$ to $\ov{X}$.
2732	For any such prolongation $\ov{E}$, the functor
2733	$\Quot_{E/X/S}$ is an open subfunctor of $\Quot_{\ov{E}/\ov{X}/S}$. 
2734	Therefore the desired result now follows from Theorem \ref{Grothendieck Quot}.
2735	\qed
2736	
2737	
2738	
2739	
2740	
2741	
2742	
2743	\bigskip
2744	
2745	
2746	\centerline{\large\bf Scheme of Morphisms}
2747	
2748	
2749	\medskip
2750	
2751	
2752	We recall the following basic facts about flatness.
2753	
2754	\begin{lemma}\label{local criterion}
2755	\textbf{(1) } Any finite-type flat morphism between noetherian schemes is open.
2756	
2757	
2758	\textbf{(2) } Let $\pi : Y \to X$ be a finite-type morphism of  
2759	noetherian schemes. Then all $y\in Y$ such that $\pi$ is
2760	flat at $y$ (that is, ${\mathcal O}_{Y,y}$ is a flat ${\mathcal O}_{X,\pi(y)}$-module)
2761	form an open subset of $Y$.
2762	
2763	
2764	\textbf{(3) } Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, and let $f:X \to S$ 
2765	and $g: Y\to S$ be finite type flat morphisms.
2766	Let $\pi : Y \to X$ be any morphism such that $g = f\circ \pi$. 
2767	Let $y\in Y$, let $x=\pi(y)$, and let
2768	$s = g(y) = f(x)$. If the restricted 
2769	morphism $\pi_s : Y_s \to X_s$ between the 
2770	fibers over $s$ is flat at $y\in Y_s$, then $\pi$ is flat at $y\in Y$. 
2771	\end{lemma}
2772	
2773	\proof See for example Altman and Kleiman [A-K 1] Chapter V. 
2774	The statement \textbf{(3) } is a consequence of what is known as the 
2775	\textbf{local criterion for flatness}. \qed
2776	
2777	
2778	\begin{theorem}\label{isomorphism is open}
2779	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, and let $f:X \to S$ 
2780	and $g: Y\to S$ be proper flat morphisms.
2781	Let $\pi : Y \to X$ be any projective morphism with
2782	$g = f\circ \pi$. Then $S$ has open subschemes
2783	$S_2\subset S_1 \subset S$ with the following universal properties:
2784	
2785	\textbf{(a) } For any locally noetherian $S$-scheme $T$, the base change
2786	$\pi_T : Y_T \to X_T$ is a flat morphism if and only if the 
2787	structure morphism $T\to S$ factors via $S_1$. (This does not need
2788	$\pi$ to be projective.)
2789	
2790	
2791	\textbf{(b) } For any locally noetherian $S$-scheme $T$, the base change
2792	$\pi_T : Y_T \to X_T$ is an isomorphism if and only if the 
2793	structure morphism $T\to S$ factors via $S_2$.
2794	\end{theorem}
2795	
2796	\proof \textbf{(a) } By Lemma \ref{local criterion}.(2), all
2797	$y\in Y$ such that $\pi$ is flat at $y$ form an
2798	open subset $Y'\subset Y$. Then $S_1 = S - g(Y - Y')$ 
2799	is an open subset of $S$ as $g$ is proper. 
2800	We give $S_1$ the open subscheme structure induced from $S$.
2801	It follows from the local criterion of flatness 
2802	(Lemma  \ref{local criterion}.(3))
2803	that $S_1$ exactly consists of all $s\in S$ such that 
2804	the restricted morphism $\pi_s : Y_s \to X_s$ between the 
2805	fibers over $s$ is flat. Therefore again by the 
2806	local criterion of flatness, $S_1$ has the desired universal property.
2807	
2808	\textbf{(b) } Let $\pi_1 : Y_1 \to X_1$ be the pull-back of
2809	$\pi$ under the inclusion $S_1 \hra S$.
2810	Let $L$ be a relatively very ample 
2811	line bundle for the projective morphism $\pi_1 : Y_1\to X_1$. 
2812	Then by noetherianness there exists an integer $m\ge 1$ such that
2813	${\pi_1}_*L^m$ is generated by its global sections and $R^i{\pi_1}_*L^m =0$
2814	for all $i \ge 1$. By flatness of $\pi_1$, it follows that 
2815	${\pi_1}_*L^m$ is a locally free sheaf. Let $U\subset X_1$ be the
2816	open subschemes such that 
2817	${\pi_1}_*L^m$ is of rank $1$ on $U$. 
2818	Finally, let $S_2 = S_1 - f(X_1 - U)$, which is open as
2819	$f$ is proper. 
2820	We give $S_2$ the induced open subscheme structure, 
2821	and leave it to the reader to verify that 
2822	it indeed has the required universal property \textbf{(b) }. \qed
2823	
2824	
2825	
2826	
2827	\medskip
2828	
2829	If $X$ and $Y$ are schemes over a base $S$, then for any
2830	$S$-scheme $T$, 
2831	an \textbf{$S$-morphism from $X$ to $Y$ parametrised by $T$} 
2832	will mean a $T$-morphism from $X\times_ST$ to $Y\times_ST$.
2833	The set of all such will be denoted by $\Mor_S(X,Y)(T)$.
2834	The association $T\mapsto  \Mor_S(X,Y)(T)$
2835	defines a contravariant functor $\Mor_S(X,Y)$ from $S$-schemes to $Sets$. 
2836	
2837	
2838	\medskip
2839	
2840	
2841	
2842	\textbf{Exercise } Let $k$ be a field, let $S=\spec k[[t]]$, 
2843	$X= \spec k = \spec (k[[t]]/(t))$, and let $Y = \PP^1_S$.
2844	Is $\Mor_S(X,Y)$ representable?
2845	
2846	
2847	
2848	
2849	\medskip
2850	
2851	
2852	
2853	\begin{theorem}
2854	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, let $X$ be a projective scheme over $S$,
2855	and let $Y$ be quasi-projective scheme
2856	over $S$. Assume moreover that $X$ is flat over $S$. Then the
2857	functor $\Mor_S(X,Y)$ is representable by an open
2858	subscheme $\mor_S(X,Y)$ of $\hilb_{X\times_SY/S}$.
2859	\end{theorem}
2860	
2861	\proof We can associate
2862	to each morphism $f : X_T \to Y_T$ (where $T$ is a scheme over $S$)
2863	its graph $\Gamma_T(f) \subset (X\times_SY)_T$, which is
2864	closed in $(X\times_SY)_T$ by separatedness of $Y\to S$.
2865	We regard $\Gamma_T(f)$ as a closed subscheme of $(X\times_SY)_T$ 
2866	which is isomorphic to $X$ under the graph morphism
2867	$(\id_X,f): X \to  \Gamma_T(f)$ and the projection $\Gamma_T(f) \to X$,
2868	which are inverses to each other.
2869	As $X$ is proper and flat over $S$, so is $\Gamma_T(f)$, therefore
2870	this defines a set-map $\Gamma_T : \Mor_S(X,Y)(T) \to 
2871	\Hilb_{X\times_SY/S}(T)$ which is functorial in $T$, so
2872	we obtain a morphism of functors 
2873	$$\Gamma : \Mor_S(X,Y) \to \Hilb_{X\times_SY/S}$$ 
2874	
2875	
2876	Given any element of $\Hilb_{X\times_SY/S}(T)$, represented by a
2877	family $Z \subset (X\times_SY)_T$, it follows by applying 
2878	Theorem \ref{isomorphism is open}.(b) to
2879	the projection $Z\to X$ that $T$ has an open subscheme
2880	$T'$ with the following universal property: for any
2881	base-change $U \to T$, the pull-back $Z_U\subset (X\times_SY)_U$
2882	maps isomorphically on to $X_U$ under the projection
2883	$p : (X\times_SY)_U\to X_U$ if and only if $U\to T$ factors via
2884	$T'$. Note therefore that over $T'$, 
2885	the scheme $Z_{T'}$ will be the graph of a uniquely determined 
2886	morphism $X_{T'} \to Y_{T'}$. 
2887	
2888	This shows that the morphism of functors 
2889	$\Gamma : \Mor_S(X,Y) \to \Hilb_{X\times_SY/S}$
2890	is a representable morphism which is an open embedding. 
2891	Therefore a representing scheme
2892	$\mor_S(X,Y)$ for $\Mor_S(X,Y)$ exists as an open
2893	subscheme of $\hilb_{X\times_SY/S}$. \qed
2894	
2895	
2896	
2897	
2898	\textbf{Exercise } Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and
2899	$X \to S$ a flat projective morphism. 
2900	Consider the set-valued contravariant functor $\Aut_{X/S}$ 
2901	on locally noetherian $S$-schemes, which associates
2902	to any $T$ the set of all automorphisms of $X_T$ over $T$.
2903	Show that this functor is representable by an open subscheme of
2904	$\mor_S(X,X)$.
2905	
2906	
2907	
2908	\medskip
2909	
2910	
2911	
2912	\textbf{Exercise } Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme 
2913	and $\pi : Z \to X$ a morphism of $S$-schemes,
2914	where $X$ is proper over $S$ and $Z$ is quasi-projective over $S$.
2915	Consider the set-valued contravariant functor 
2916	$\Pi_{Z/X/S}$ on locally noetherian $S$-schemes, which associates
2917	to any $T$ the set of all sections of $\pi_T : Z_T \to X_T$. 
2918	Show that this functor is representable by an open subscheme of
2919	$\hilb_{Z/S}$.
2920

2926
