2928	
2929	\medskip
2930	
2931	\bigskip
2932	
2933	\centerline{\large\bf Quotient by a Flat Projective Equivalence Relation}
2934	
2935	
2936	
2937	\medskip
2938	
2939	
2940	Let $X$ be a scheme over a base $S$.
2941	A \textbf{schematic equivalence relation} on $X$ over $S$ will
2942	mean an $S$-scheme $R$ together with a morphism
2943	$f: R \to X\times_SX$ over $S$ such that for any $S$-scheme
2944	$T$ the set map $f(T) : R(T) \to X(T)\times X(T)$
2945	is injective and its image is the graph of an equivalence relation on 
2946	$X(T)$. (Here, we denote by $Z(T)$ the set $Mor_S(T,Z)= h_Z(T)$ of
2947	all $T$-valued points of $Z$, where $Z$ and $T$ are $S$-schemes.)
2948	
2949	
2950	We will say that a morphism $q : X\to Q$ of $S$-schemes  
2951	is a \textbf{quotient} for a 
2952	schematic equivalence relation $f: R \to X\times_SX$ over $S$ if $q$
2953	is a \textbf{co-equaliser} for the component morphisms
2954	$f_1,f_2 : R  \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} X$ of 
2955	$f: R \to X\times_SX$. This means $q \circ f_1 = q\circ f_2$, 
2956	and given any $S$-scheme $Z$ and an $S$-morphism $g : X \to Z$ such that
2957	$g\circ f_1 = g\circ f_2$, there exists a unique $S$-morphism
2958	$h : Q \to Z$ such that $g = h\circ q$. 
2959	A schematic quotient $q: X\to Q$, when it exists, is
2960	unique up to a unique isomorphism. 
2961	\textbf{Exercise:} A schematic quotient, when it exists,
2962	is necessarily an epimorphism in the category of $S$-schemes.
2963	
2964	
2965	
2966	\medskip
2967	
2968	\textbf{Caution } Even if $q: X\to Q$ is a schematic quotient for $R$, 
2969	for a given $T$ the map $q(T) : X(T) \to Q(T)$ may not be a quotient
2970	for $R(T)$ in the category of sets. The map $q(T)$ may fail to 
2971	be surjective, and moreover it may identify two distinct equivalence classes.
2972	\textbf{Exercise:} Give examples where such phenomena occur.
2973	
2974	
2975	\medskip
2976	
2977	
2978	We will say that the quotient $q : X\to Q$ is \textbf{effective}
2979	if the induced morphism $(f_1,f_2) : R \to X\times_QX$ is an isomorphism
2980	of $S$-schemes. 
2981	In particular, it will ensure that distinct equivalence classes
2982	do not get identified under $q(T) : X(T) \to Q(T)$. But 
2983	$q(T)$ can still fail to be surjective, as in the
2984	following example.
2985	
2986	
2987	\medskip
2988	
2989	
2990	\textbf{Exercise } Let 
2991	$S = \spec \Z$, and let $X \subset \AA^n_{\Z}$ be 
2992	the complement of the zero section of $\AA^n_{\Z}$. 
2993	Note that for any ring $B$, an 
2994	element of $X(\spec B)$ is a vector 
2995	$u\in B^n$ such that at least one component of $u$ is invertible in
2996	$B$. Show that $X\times_SX$ has a closed subscheme $R$
2997	whose $B$-valued points for any ring $B$ are all pairs 
2998	$(u,v)\in X(\spec B)\times X(\spec B)$ such that
2999	there exists an invertible element $\lambda \in B^{\times}$
3000	with $\lambda u = v$. Show that an effective quotient $q: X\to Q$ exists, 
3001	where $Q = \PP^{n-1}_{\Z}$. However, show that 
3002	$q$ does not admit a global section, and so 
3003	$q(Q) : X(Q) \to Q(Q)$ is not surjective.
3004	
3005	\medskip
3006	
3007	The famous example by Hironaka 
3008	(see Example 3.4.1 in Hartshorne [H] Appendix B)
3009	of a non-projective smooth complete variety $X$ 
3010	over $\C$ together with a schematic equivalence relation $R$ 
3011	(for which the morphisms $f_i : R\to X$ are finite flat,
3012	in fact, \'etale of degree $2$) shows that 
3013	schematic quotients do not always exist. 
3014	But under the powerful assumption of projectivity,
3015	Grothendieck proved an existence result for quotients, to which we
3016	devote the rest of this section.
3017	
3018	We will need the following elementary lemma from Grothendieck's 
3019	theory of faithfully flat descent (this is a special case of
3020	[SGA 1] Expos\'e VIII Corollary 1.9). 
3021	The reader can consult the lectures of Vistoli
3022	[V] for an exposition of descent.
3023	
3024	\begin{lemma}\label{descent of subschemes}
3025	(1) Any faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism of schemes $f : X \to Y$ 
3026	is an effective epimorphism, that is, $f$ is a co-equaliser for
3027	the projections $p_1,p_2: X\times_YX   \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} X$.
3028	
3029	(2) Let $p : D\to H$ be a faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism.
3030	Let $Z \subset D$ be a closed subscheme
3031	such that 
3032	$$p_1^{-1}Z = p_2^{-1}Z \subset D\times_HD$$ 
3033	where $p_1,p_2 :  D\times_HD \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} D$ 
3034	are the projections, and $p_i^{-1}Z$ is the schematic 
3035	inverse image of $Z$ under $p_i$. 
3036	Then there exists a unique closed subscheme $Q$ of $H$
3037	such that $Z = p^{-1}Q \subset D$. By base-change from $p : D\to H$, 
3038	it follows that the induced morphism $p|_Z : Z\to Q$ 
3039	is faithfully flat and quasi-compact.\hfill $\square$
3040	\end{lemma}
3041	
3042	
3043	The idea of using Hilbert schemes to make
3044	quotients of flat projective equivalence relations is due to Grothendieck, 
3045	who used it in his construction of a relative Picard scheme.
3046	In set-theoretic terms, the idea is actually very simple:
3047	Let $X$ be a set,
3048	and $R\subset X\times X$ an equivalence relation on $X$. 
3049	Let $H$ be the power set of $X$ (means the set of all subsets of $X$),
3050	and let ${\varphi}: X \to H$ be the map which sends $x\in X$ to its
3051	equivalence class $[x] \in H$. If $Q\subset H$ is the image of ${\varphi}$, 
3052	then the induced map $q: X\to Q$ is the quotient of $X$ modulo $R$ in the 
3053	category of sets. The scheme-theoretic analogue of the above is the
3054	following theorem of Grothendieck, where the Hilbert scheme of $X$ 
3055	plays the role of power set. The first detailed proof appeared in
3056	Altman and Kleiman [A-K 2].
3057	
3058	
3059	\begin{theorem}\label{Projective flat quotient}
3060	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, and let
3061	$X\to S$ be a quasi-projective morphism.
3062	Let $f : R \to X\times_SX$ be a schematic 
3063	equivalence relation on $X$ over $S$, such that 
3064	the projections $f_1,f_2 : R \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} X$  
3065	are proper and flat. 
3066	Then a schematic quotient $X\to Q$ exists over $S$. 
3067	Moreover, $Q$ is quasi-projective over $S$,
3068	the morphism $X \to Q$ is faithfully flat and projective, and 
3069	the induced morphism $(f_1,f_2) : R\to X\times_QX$ is an isomorphism (the
3070	quotient is effective). 
3071	\end{theorem}
3072	
3073	
3074	\proof (Following Altman and Kleiman [A-K 2]) 
3075	The properness of $f_i$
3076	together with separatedness of $X\to S$ implies properness of
3077	$f : R \to X\times_SX$. 
3078	Also, $f$ is functorially injective by definition
3079	of a schematic equivalence relation. 
3080	It follows that $f$ is a closed embedding, which
3081	allows us to regard $R$ as a closed subscheme of $X\times_SX$ 
3082	(\textbf{Exercise}: 
3083	\textit{Any proper morphism of noetherian schemes, which is injective 
3084	at the level of functor of points, is a closed embedding}). 
3085	This defines an element $(R)$ of $\Hilb_{X/S}(X)$, as the projection
3086	$p_2|_R = f_2$ is proper and flat.
3087	
3088	
3089	
3090	
3091	By Theorem \ref{Quasi-projective quot}, 
3092	there exists a scheme $\hilb_{X/S}$ which represents the
3093	functor $\Hilb_{X/S}$. 
3094	As the parameter scheme $X$ is noetherian and as the Hilbert polynomial
3095	is locally constant, 
3096	only finitely many polynomials $\Phi$ occur as Hilbert 
3097	polynomials of fibers of $f_2: R \to X$ with respect to a
3098	chosen relatively very ample line bundle $L$ on $X$ over $S$.
3099	Let $H$ be the finite disjoint union of the corresponding 
3100	open subschemes  
3101	$\hilb_{X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ of $\hilb_{X/S}$.
3102	Then $H$ is a quasi-projective scheme over $S$ as 
3103	each $\hilb_{X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is so by 
3104	Theorem \ref{Quasi-projective quot}. 
3105	The family $(R) \in  \Hilb_{X/S}(X)$
3106	therefore defines a classifying morphism 
3107	${\varphi}: X \to H$, with the property that $\varphi^*D = R$ 
3108	where $D\subset X\times_SH$ denotes the restriction to $H$ 
3109	of the universal family over $\hilb_{X/S}$,
3110	and $\varphi^*D$ denotes $(\id_X\times\,{\varphi})^{-1} D$.
3111	Also, note that the projection $p: D\to H$ is proper and flat.
3112	If $X$ is non-empty then each fiber of $f_2 : R \to X$
3113	is also non-empty as the diagonal $\Delta_X$ is contained in $R$,
3114	and therefore the Hilbert polynomial of each fiber is non-zero.
3115	Hence $p: D\to H$ is surjective, and so $p$ is faithfully flat.
3116	
3117	
3118	For any $S$-scheme $T$, it follows from its definition that
3119	a $T$-valued point of $D$ is a pair $(x,V)$ with
3120	$x\in X(T)$ and $V \in H(T)$ such that
3121	$$x\in V$$
3122	where the notation ``$x\in V$'' more precisely means that
3123	the graph morphism $(x, \id_T) : T \to X\times_ST$ 
3124	factors via $V \subset X\times_ST$. With this notation,
3125	we will establish the following crucial property:
3126	
3127	
3128	\medskip
3129	
3130	
3131	
3132	\textbf{(***)} \textit{For any $S$-scheme $T$, and 
3133	$T$-valued points $x,y \in X(T)$,
3134	the following equivalences hold:}
3135	$(x,y) \in R(T) \Leftrightarrow x \in \varphi(y)
3136	\Leftrightarrow \varphi(x) = \varphi(y) \in H(T)$.
3137	
3138	
3139	
3140	\medskip
3141	
3142	
3143	For this, note that for any $x,y\in X(T)$, the morphism 
3144	$(x,y): T \to X\times_SX$  factors as the composite
3145	$$T \stackrel{(x,\id_T)}{\to}X\times_S T 
3146	\stackrel{\id_X \times\, y}{\to}  X\times_S X$$
3147	As $\varphi^*D = R$ and $(\varphi\circ y)^*D = \varphi(y)$,
3148	it follows that $y^*R =  \varphi(y)$, in other words,
3149	the schematic inverse image of $R\subset X\times_S X$ under 
3150	$\id_X\times\, y : X\times_S T \to X\times_S X$
3151	is $\varphi(y)\subset X\times_S T$. Hence the above factorisation
3152	of $(x,y) : T\to X\times_SX$ shows that 
3153	\begin{eqnarray*}
3154	&& (x,y) \in R(T) \\
3155	& \Leftrightarrow 
3156	& \mbox{~the~morphism~} (x,\id_T):T \to X\times_ST 
3157	  \mbox{~factors~via~} \varphi(y)\subset X\times_ST \\
3158	& \Leftrightarrow & x \in \varphi(y)
3159	\end{eqnarray*}
3160	Moreover, $x \in \varphi(x)$ as $\Delta_X \subset R$.
3161	Therefore, if $\varphi(x) = \varphi(y)$ then
3162	$x \in \varphi(y)$. 
3163	
3164	It now only remains to prove that 
3165	if $(x,y) \in R(T)$ then $\varphi(x) = \varphi(y)$,
3166	that is, the subschemes 
3167	$\varphi(x)$ and $\varphi(y)$ of $X\times_S T$ are identical.
3168	Note that 
3169	$\varphi(x) = (\varphi\circ x)^*D = x^*\varphi^*D = 
3170	x^*R$ and similarly $\varphi(y) = y^*R$, therefore
3171	we wish to show that $x^*R  = y^*R$. To show this
3172	in terms of functor of points, for any $T$-scheme $u: U \to T$  
3173	we just have to show that
3174	$(x^*R)(U) = (y^*R)(U)$ as subsets of $(X\times_ST)(U)$.
3175	As $x^*R$ is the inverse image of $R$ under 
3176	$\id_X\times\, x : X\times_ST \to X\times_SX$, it follows that
3177	a $U$-valued point of $x^*R$ is the same as
3178	an element $z\in X(U)$
3179	such that $(\id_X\times\, x)\circ (z,u) \in R(U)$.
3180	But as $(\id_X\times\, x)\circ (z,u) = (z, x\circ u)$, it follows that
3181	$$z \in (x^*R)(U) \Leftrightarrow (z, x\circ u) \in R(U)$$
3182	As $R(U)$ is an equivalence relation on the set $X(U)$, and 
3183	as by assumption $(x,y) \in R(T)$, we have 
3184	$(x\circ u, y\circ u) \in R(U)$, and so by transitivity 
3185	we have
3186	$$z \in (x^*R)(U) \Leftrightarrow (z, x\circ u) \in R(U)
3187	\Leftrightarrow (z, y\circ u) \in R(U) 
3188	\Leftrightarrow z \in (y^*R)(U)$$
3189	Hence the subschemes $x^*R$ and $y^*R$ of $X\times_ST$
3190	have the same $U$-valued points
3191	for any $T$-scheme $U$, and therefore $x^*R = y^*R$, as was to be shown. 
3192	This completes the proof of the assertion \textbf{(***)}.
3193	
3194	
3195	
3196	
3197	
3198	
3199	The graph morphism $(\id_X, {\varphi}) : X \to X\times_S H$
3200	is a closed embedding as $H$ is separated over $S$.
3201	As $\Delta_X \subset R$ and as 
3202	${\varphi}^* D = R$, it follows that
3203	$(\id_X, {\varphi})$ factors through $D\subset X\times_SH$.
3204	Thus, we get a closed subscheme $\Gamma_{\varphi} \subset D$,
3205	which is the isomorphic image of $X$ under 
3206	$(\id_X, {\varphi})$. We wish to apply the 
3207	Lemma \ref{descent of subschemes}.(2)
3208	to the faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism 
3209	$p: D \to H$ and the closed subscheme $Z = \Gamma_{\varphi} \subset D$.
3210	
3211	Any $T$-valued point of 
3212	$\Gamma_{\varphi}$ is a pair $(x, {\varphi}(x)) \in D(T)$ where  
3213	$x\in X(T)$. Any $T$-valued point of $D\times_HD$ is a triple
3214	$(x,y,V)$ where $x,y\in X(T)$ and $V \in H(T)$ 
3215	such that $x, y \in V$.
3216	Under the projections
3217	$p_1,p_2 :  D\times_HD \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} D$,
3218	we have $p_1(x,y,V) = (x,V)$ and $p_2(x,y,V) = (y,V)$.
3219	We now have 
3220	\begin{eqnarray*}
3221	&& p_1(x,y,V) \in \Gamma_{\varphi}(T)\\
3222	& \Leftrightarrow & (x,V) \in \Gamma_{\varphi}(T) \mbox{ and }y\in V \\
3223	& \Leftrightarrow & V = \varphi(x)\mbox{ and }y\in V \\
3224	& \Leftrightarrow & y \in \varphi(x) = V \\
3225	& \Leftrightarrow & \varphi(y) = \varphi(x) =V ~~(\mbox{by the property  
3226	\textbf{(***)}).}
3227	\end{eqnarray*}
3228	Similarly, we have $p_2(x,y,V) \in \Gamma_{\varphi}(T)$ if and only if
3229	$\varphi(y) = \varphi(x) =V$.
3230	Therefore, $p_1(x,y,V) \in \Gamma_{\varphi}(T)$ if and only if 
3231	$p_2(x,y,V) \in \Gamma_{\varphi}(T)$.
3232	This holds for all $T$-valued points for all $S$-schemes $T$, and so 
3233	$p_1^{-1}\Gamma_{\varphi} = p_2^{-1}\Gamma_{\varphi} \subset D\times_HD$.
3234	Therefore by Lemma \ref{descent of subschemes}(2)  
3235	there exists a unique closed subscheme $Q \subset H$ such that 
3236	$\Gamma_{\varphi}$ is the pull-back of $Q$ under $D\to H$. 
3237	Let $p : \Gamma_{\varphi} \to Q$ be the morphism induced by the restriction
3238	to $\Gamma_{\varphi}$ of $p: D\to H$. 
3239	Let $q: X\to Q$ be defined as the composite
3240	$$X \stackrel{(\id_X,\varphi)}{\to} \Gamma_{\varphi} 
3241	\stackrel{p}{\to} Q$$
3242	Then note that the composite 
3243	$X \stackrel{q}{\to} Q \hra H$ equals $\varphi$. 
3244	
3245	We will now show that $q: X\to Q$ as defined above is the desired
3246	quotient of $X$ by $R$, with the required properties.
3247	
3248	\textbf{(i) Quasi-projectivity of $Q\to S$ }: This is satisfied as
3249	$Q$ is closed in $H$ and $H$ is quasi-projective over $S$.
3250	
3251	\textbf{(ii) Faithful flatness and projectivity of $q$ }: 
3252	This follows 
3253	by base change from the faithfully flat projective morphism $p: D\to H$,
3254	as the following square is Cartesian. 
3255	$$\begin{array}{ccc}
3256	X & \stackrel{(\id_X,\varphi)}{\to} & D \\
3257	{\scriptstyle q} \dna ~ & \square & ~ \dna {\scriptstyle p} \\
3258	Q & \hra & H
3259	\end{array}$$
3260	
3261	\textbf{(iii) Exactness of $R \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} X \to Q$
3262	and the isomorphism $R \to X\times_QX$ }:
3263	By \textbf{(***)}, for any $T$-valued points $x,y \in X(T)$, we have
3264	$(x,y)\in R(T)$ if and only if $\varphi(x) = \varphi(y)$.
3265	This shows that the composite $R \stackrel{f_1}{\to} X \stackrel{q}{\to} Q$
3266	equals the composite $R \stackrel{f_2}{\to} X \stackrel{q}{\to} Q$, and
3267	the induced morphism $(f_1,f_2) : R \to X\times_QX$ 
3268	is an isomorphism, by showing these statements
3269	hold at the level of functor of points. Under the isomorphism
3270	$R \to X\times_QX$, the 
3271	morphisms $f_1,f_2 : R  \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} X$
3272	become the projection morphisms $p_1,p_2 : X\times_QX
3273	\stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} X$.
3274	By Lemma \ref{descent of subschemes}, the
3275	morphism $q: X \to Q$ is a co-equaliser for $p_1,p_2$, and so $q$
3276	is a co-equaliser for $f_1,f_2$. 
3277	
3278	This completes the proof of Theorem \ref{Projective flat quotient}.\qed
3279	
3280	
3281	
3282	What Altman and Kleiman actually prove in [A-K 2] is 
3283	a strongly projective form of the above theorem
3284	(without a noetherian assumption), 
3285	using the hypothesis of strong quasi-projectivity 
3286	in the following places in the above proof: if $X\to S$ is
3287	strongly quasi-projective then $H \to S$ will again be so
3288	by Theorem \ref{Strongly quasi-projective quot}, and therefore
3289	$Q$ will be strongly quasi-projective over $S$. Moreover, $D\to H$
3290	will be strongly projective, and therefore by base-change
3291	$X\to Q$ will be strongly projective. 
3292	In the noetherian case, this gives us the following result.
3293	
3294	
3295	
3296	\begin{theorem}
3297	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, and let
3298	$X\to S$ be a strongly quasi-projective morphism.
3299	Let $f : R \to X\times_SX$ be a schematic 
3300	equivalence relation on $X$ over $S$, such that 
3301	the projections $f_1,f_2 : R \stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} X$  
3302	are proper and flat. 
3303	Then a schematic quotient $X\to Q$ exists over $S$. 
3304	Moreover, the quotient is effective, 
3305	the morphism $X \to Q$ is
3306	faithfully flat and strongly projective, and $Q$ is strongly
3307	quasi-projective over $S$. 
3308	\end{theorem}

287	\section{The Hilbert and Quot Functors}
288	
289	
290	
291	
292	
293	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$}
294	
295	
296	\medskip
297	
298	The main problem addressed in this series of lectures, in its simplest
299	form, is as follows. If $S$ is a locally noetherian scheme, 
300	a \textbf{family of subschemes of $\PP^n$ parametrised by $S$} will mean
301	a closed subscheme $Y \subset \PP^n_S = \PP^n_{\Z}\times S$ 
302	such that $Y$ is flat over $S$. 
303	If $f: T\to S$ is any morphism of locally noetherian schemes, then
304	by pull-back we get a family $f^*(Y) = (\id \times f)^{-1}(Y) 
305	\subset \PP^n_T$ 
306	parametrised by $T$, from a family $Y$ parametrised by $S$.
307	This defines a contravariant functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ 
308	from the category of all locally noetherian schemes to the category of sets, 
309	which associates to any $S$ the set of all such families
310	$$\Hilb_{\PP^n}(S) = \{ Y \subset \PP^n_S \,|\,
311	Y \mbox{ is flat over }S \}$$
312	
313	\textbf{Question:} Is the functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ representable?
314	
315	Grothendieck proved that this question has an affirmative answer, that is, 
316	there exists a locally noetherian scheme $\hilb_{\PP^n}$ together with
317	a family $Z \subset \PP^n_{\Z}\times \hilb_{\PP^n}$ 
318	parametrised by $\hilb_{\PP^n}$,
319	such that any family $Y$ over $S$ is obtained as the
320	pull-back of $Z$ 
321	by a uniquely determined morphism $\varphi_Y : S \to \hilb_{\PP^n}$.
322	In other words, $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ is isomorphic to 
323	the functor $Mor(-,\hilb_{\PP^n})$.
324	
325	
326	
327	
328	
329	\medskip
330	
331	
332	\bigskip
333	
334	
335	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$}
336	\nopagebreak
337	\medskip
338	\nopagebreak
339	A family $Y$ of subschemes of $\PP^n$ parametrised by $S$
340	is the same as 
341	a coherent quotient sheaf $q: {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S} \to {\mathcal O}_Y$ on 
342	$\PP^n_S$, such that ${\mathcal O}_Y$ is flat over $S$. 
343	This way of looking at the functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$
344	has the following fruitful generalisation. 
345	
346	
347	Let $r$ be any positive integer. 
348	A \textbf{family of quotients of $\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}$ 
349	parametrised by a locally noetherian scheme $S$} will mean 
350	a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 
351	
352	(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $\PP^n_S$ which is flat over $S$, and
353	
354	(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
355	$q:\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}  \to \F$.
356	
357	Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $S$ will 
358	be regarded as equivalent if there exists an isomorphism $f: \F \to \F'$
359	which takes $q$ to $q'$, that is, the following diagram commutes.
360	$$\begin{array}{ccc}
361	\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n} &\stackrel{q}{\to} & \F \\
362	\|                  &     & ~ \dna {\scriptstyle f} \\
363	\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n} &\stackrel{q'}{\to} & \F' 
364	\end{array}$$
365	This is the same as the condition 
366	$\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. We will denote by $\langle \F, q \rangle$ 
367	an equivalence class. If $f : T\to S$ is a morphism of 
368	locally noetherian schemes, then pulling back the quotient
369	$q: \oplus^r{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S} \to \F$ 
370	under $\id\times f : \PP^n_T \to \PP^n_S$
371	defines a family $f^*(q) : \oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_T} \to f^*(\F)$ over $T$, 
372	which makes sense as tensor product is right-exact and preserves flatness. 
373	The operation of pulling back respects equivalence of families,
374	therefore it gives rise to a contravariant functor
375	$\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$ from the category of
376	all locally noetherian schemes to the category of sets, 
377	by putting 
378	$$\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}(S) = 
379	\{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }S\}$$
380	
381	
382	It is immediate that the functor $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
383	satisfies faithfully flat descent. It was proved by
384	Grothendieck that in fact the above functor
385	is representable on the category of all locally noetherian schemes 
386	by a scheme $\quot_{\oplus^r{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$. 
387	
388	
389	\medskip
390	
391	
392	\bigskip
393	
394	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and $\Quot_{E/X/S}$}
395	
396	\medskip
397	
398	The above functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ and $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
399	admit the following simple generalisations. 
400	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $X\to S$ be a finite type
401	scheme over it. Let $E$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$. 
402	Let $Sch_S$ denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
403	over $S$. For any $T\to S$ in $Sch_S$, a \textbf{family of quotients of $E$ 
404	parametrised by $T$} will mean 
405	a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 
406	
407	(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$ 
408	such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$ 
409	and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with 
410	
411	(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{X_T}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
412	$q:E_T  \to \F$ where $E_T$ is the pull-back of $E$ under
413	the projection $X_T \to X$.
414	
415	Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $T$ will 
416	be regarded as equivalent if $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$), 
417	and $\langle \F, q \rangle$ will denote 
418	an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
419	by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact, 
420	the pull-back of $\langle \F, q \rangle$ under an $S$-morphism $T'\to T$ is
421	well-defined, which gives
422	a set-valued contravariant functor 
423	$\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ under which   
424	$$T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}$$
425	
426	When $E = {\mathcal O}_X$, the functor $\Quot_{{\mathcal O}_X/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ 
427	associates to $T$ the set of all closed subschemes $Y\subset X_T$
428	that are proper and flat over $T$. We denote this functor by $\Hilb_{X/S}$.
429	
430	Note in particular that we have
431	$$\Hilb_{\PP^n} = \Hilb_{\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z} \mbox{ and }
432	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}} = 
433	\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}}/\PP^n_{\Z}/\spec \Z}$$ 
434	
435	
436	It is clear that the functors $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ and $\Hilb_{X/S}$
437	satisfy faithfully flat descent, 
438	so it makes sense to pose the question of their
439	representability. 
440	
441	
442	
443	\medskip
444	
445	
446	\bigskip
447	
448	\centerline{\large\bf Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials}
449	
450	\medskip
451	
452	
453	Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
454	a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
455	Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on 
456	$X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
457	the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
458	the function 
459	$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) = 
460	\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
461	where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the 
462	coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
463	is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
464	case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
465	
466	
467	
468	Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
469	and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
470	be any coherent sheaf on 
471	$X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
472	we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$ 
473	which is the Hilbert polynomial of
474	the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
475	calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$. 
476	If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$ 
477	from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is 
478	known to be locally constant on $S$. 
479	
480	
481	This shows that the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ naturally decomposes
482	as a co-product 
483	$$\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
484	where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor 
485	$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ associates
486	to any $T$ the set of all equivalence classes of 
487	families $\langle \F,q \rangle$ such that at each $t\in T$ the 
488	Hilbert polynomial of the restriction $\F_t$, calculated
489	using the pull-back of $L$, is ${\Phi}$. Correspondingly,
490	the representing scheme  $\quot_{E/X/S}$, when it exists, naturally decomposes
491	as a co-product
492	$$\quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
493	
494	\textbf{Note } 
495	We will generally take $X$ to be (quasi-)projective over $S$,
496	and $L$ to be a relatively very ample line bundle. Then indeed 
497	the Hilbert and Quot functors are representable by schemes,
498	but not in general.
499	
500	
501	
502	\medskip
503	
504	
505	\bigskip
506	
507	\centerline{\large\bf Elementary Examples, Exercises}
508	
509	
510	\medskip
511	
512	\textbf{(1) } {\bf $\PP^n_{\Z}$ as a Quot scheme } 
513	Show that the scheme $\PP^n_{\Z} = \proj \Z[x_0,\ldots,x_n]$ represents the 
514	functor $\varphi$ from schemes to sets, which associates
515	to any $S$ the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ 
516	of quotients
517	$q: \oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_S \to \F$, where $\F$ is an invertible ${\mathcal O}_S$-module. 
518	As coherent sheaves on $S$ which are ${\mathcal O}_S$-flat with each
519	fiber $1$-dimensional are exactly the locally free sheaves on $S$ 
520	of rank $1$, it follows that $\varphi$ is the functor 
521	$\Quot_{\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{1, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
522	(where in some places we write just $\Z$ for $\spec \Z$ for simplicity). 
523	This shows that
524	$\quot_{\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{1, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}} 
525	= \PP^n_{\Z}$. 
526	Under this identification, show that the universal family on
527	$\quot_{\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{1, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$ 
528	is the tautological quotient 
529	$\oplus^{n+1}{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}} \to {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_{\Z}}(1)$
530	 
531	More generally, show that if $E$ is a locally free sheaf on a 
532	noetherian scheme $S$, the functor $\Quot_{E/S/S}^{1, {\mathcal O}_S}$ is represented 
533	by the $S$-scheme $\PP(E) = \Proj \sym_{{\mathcal O}_S} E$, 
534	with the tautological quotient 
535	$\pi^*(E) \to {\mathcal O}_{\PP(E)}(1)$ as the universal family.
536	
537	
538	
539	
540	
541	
542	\medskip
543	
544	
545	
546	\textbf{(2) } \textbf{Grassmannian as a Quot scheme } 
547	For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, an explicit construction
548	the Grassmannian scheme
549	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological 
550	quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
551	$\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$, 
552	has been given at the end of this section. A proof of the 
553	properness of $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is given there, 
554	together with a closed embedding $\grass(r,d)\hra 
555	\PP(\pi_*\det\U) = \PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m={r \choose d} - 1$.
556	
557	Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient 
558	$u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the 
559	contravariant functor 
560	$$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$ 
561	from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
562	set of all equivalence classes 
563	$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
564	where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
565	Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$ 
566	exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$.
567	
568	
569	\textbf{Grassmannian of a vector bundle} 
570	Show that for any ring
571	$A$, the action of the group $GL_r(A)$ on the free module $\oplus^r A$ 
572	induces an action of $GL_r(A)$ on 
573	the set $\Grass(r,d)(A)$, such that for any ring homomorphism $A\to B$,
574	the set-map $\Grass(r,d)(A)\to\Grass(r,d)(B)$ is equivariant with respect
575	to the group homomorphism $GL_r(A) \to GL_r(B)$. (In schematic terms,
576	this means we have an action of the group-scheme $GL_{r,\Z}$ on 
577	$\grass(r,d)$.) 
578	
579	Using the above show that, more generally, if $S$ is a scheme and 
580	$E$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_S$-module of rank $r$, 
581	the functor $\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}$ 
582	on all $S$-schemes which 
583	by definition associates to any $T$ 
584	the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients  
585	$q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,
586	is representable. The representing scheme
587	is denoted by $\grass(E,d)$ and is called the rank $d$ 
588	relative Grassmannian of $E$ over $S$.
589	It parametrises a universal quotient $\pi^*E \to \F$
590	where $\pi : \grass(E,d) \to S$ is the projection.
591	Show that the determinant line bundle $\bigwedge^d \F$ on
592	$\grass(E,d)$ is relatively very ample over $S$, 
593	and it gives a closed embedding
594	$\grass(E,d) \hra \PP(\pi_* \bigwedge^d \F) \subset \PP(\bigwedge^d E)$. 
595	(The properness of the embedding follows from the 
596	properness of $\pi : \grass(E,d) \to S$, which follows locally
597	over $S$ by base-change from  properness of 
598	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$ -- see Exercise \textbf{(5)}
599	or \textbf{(7)} below.)
600	
601	
602	\textbf{Grassmannian of a coherent sheaf} 
603	If $E$ is a coherent sheaf on $S$, not necessarily locally free, 
604	then by definition the functor $\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}$ 
605	on all $S$-schemes associates to any $T$ 
606	the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients  
607	$q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free on $T$ of rank $d$.
608	If $r: E' \to E$ is a surjection of coherent sheaves on $S$, then show that
609	the induced morphism of functors $\Grass(E,d)\to \Grass(E',d)$,
610	which sends $\langle \F, q\rangle \mapsto \langle \F, q\circ r\rangle$,
611	is a closed embedding. 
612	From this, by locally expressing a coherent
613	sheaf as a quotient of a vector bundle, show that $\Grass(E,d)$ 
614	is representable even when $E$ is a coherent sheaf on $S$ which is not 
615	necessarily locally free. The representing scheme $\grass(E,d)$ is proper
616	over $S$, as locally over $S$ it is a closed
617	subscheme of the Grassmannian of a vector bundle. 
618	Show by arguing locally over $S$ that the line bundle
619	$\bigwedge^d \F$ on $\grass(E,d)$ is relatively very ample over $S$, 
620	and therefore by using properness 
621	conclude that $\grass(E,d)$ is projective over $S$.
622	
623	
624	\medskip
625	
626	\textbf{(3) } \textbf{Grassmannian as a Hilbert scheme }
627	Let $\Phi = 1 \in \Q[\lambda]$. Then the Hilbert scheme
628	$\hilb_{\PP^n}^{1,{\mathcal O}(1)}$ is $\PP^n_{\Z}$ itself.
629	More generally, let $\Phi_r = {r + \lambda \choose r}\in \Q[\lambda]$
630	where $r \ge 0$. 
631	The Hilbert scheme $\hilb_{\PP^n}^{\Phi_r,{\mathcal O}(1)}$ is isomorphic to the 
632	Grassmannian scheme $\grass(n+1, r+1)$ over $\Z$. 
633	This can be seen via the following steps, whose detailed verification 
634	is left to the reader as an exercise.
635	
636	(i) The Grassmannian scheme 
637	$\grass(n+1, r+1)$ over $\Z$ parametrises a tautological family of 
638	subschemes of $\PP^n$ with Hilbert polynomial $\Phi_r$. 
639	Therefore we get a natural transformation 
640	$h_{\grass(n+1, r+1)} \to \Hilb_{\PP^n}^{\Phi_r,{\mathcal O}(1)}$.
641	
642	
643	(ii) Any closed subscheme $Y \subset \PP^n_k$
644	with Hilbert polynomial ${\Phi}_r$, where $k$ is any field, 
645	is isomorphic to $\PP^r_k$ embedded linearly in $\PP^n_k$ over $k$.
646	If $V$ is a vector bundle over a noetherian base $S$, and if
647	$Y\subset \PP(V)$ is a closed subscheme flat over $S$ with each
648	schematic fiber $Y_s$ an $r$-dimensional 
649	linear subspace of the projective space
650	$\PP(V_s)$, then $Y$ is defines
651	a rank $r+1$ quotient vector bundle 
652	$V = \pi_*{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1) \to \pi_*{\mathcal O}_Y(1)$  
653	where $\pi : \PP(V) \to S$ denotes the projection. 
654	This gives a natural transformation
655	$\Hilb_{\PP^n}^{{\Phi}_r,{\mathcal O}(1)} \to h_{\grass(n+1, r+1)}$.
656	
657	(iii) The above two natural transformations are inverses of each other.
658	
659	
660	
661	\medskip
662	
663	\textbf{(4) } \textbf{Hilbert scheme of hypersurfaces in $\PP^n$ }
664	Let  
665	${\Phi}_d = {n+\lambda \choose n} - {n -d + \lambda\choose n}
666	\in \Q[\lambda]$
667	where $d\ge 1$. 
668	The Hilbert scheme $\hilb_{\PP^n}^{{\Phi}_d,{\mathcal O}(1)}$ is isomorphic to 
669	$\PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m = {n+d \choose d} - 1$. 
670	This can be seen from the following steps, which are
671	left as exercises.
672	
673	(i) Any closed subscheme $Y \subset \PP^n_k$
674	with Hilbert polynomial ${\Phi}_d$, where $k$ is any field, 
675	is a hypersurface of degree $d$ in $\PP^n_k$. \textit{Hint:}
676	If $Y \subset \PP^n_k$ is a closed subscheme with
677	Hilbert polynomial of degree $n-1$, then show that
678	the schematic closure $Z$ of the hight $1$
679	primary components is a hypersurface in $\PP^n_k$ with
680	$\deg(Z) = \deg(Y)$. 
681	
682	(ii) Any family $Y \subset \PP^n_S$ is a Cartier divisor
683	in $\PP^n_S$. It gives rise to a line subbundle 
684	$\pi_* (I_Y \otimes {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}(d)) \subset \pi_* {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}(d)$,
685	which defines a natural morphism 
686	$f_Y : S \to \PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m = {n+d \choose  d} - 1$.
687	This gives a morphism of functors 
688	$\Hilb_{\PP^n}^{{\Phi}_d}\to \PP^m$ where we denote
689	$h_{\PP^m_{\Z}}$ simply by $\PP^m$.
690	
691	(iii) The scheme $\PP^m_{\Z}$ parametrises a tautological family of 
692	hypersurfaces of degree $d$, which gives a 
693	morphism of functors 
694	$\PP^m \to \Hilb_{\PP^n}^{{\Phi}_d,{\mathcal O}(1)}$ in the reverse direction.
695	These are inverses of each other. 
696	
697	
698

701	
702	\textbf{(5) } \textbf{Base-change property of Hilbert and Quot schemes} 
703	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $X$ a finite-type scheme over
704	$S$, and $E$ a coherent sheaf on $X$. 
705	If $T\to S$ is
706	a morphism of noetherian schemes, then show that there is a natural
707	isomorphism of functors
708	$\Quot_{E_T/X_T/T} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}\times_{h_S} h_T$.
709	Consequently, if $\quot_{E/X/S}$ exists, then so does
710	$\quot_{E_T/X_T/T}$, which is naturally isomorphic to 
711	$\quot_{E/X/S}\times_ST$. One can prove a similar
712	statement involving $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$.
713	In particular, $\hilb_{X/S}$ and $\hilb_{X/S}^{\Phi,L}$, 
714	when they exist, base-change correctly.
715	
716	
717	
718	
719	
720	
721	\medskip
722	
723	\textbf{(6) } \textbf{Descent condition in the fpqc topology}
724	If $U$ is an $S$-scheme and $(f_i: U_i \to U)$ is an open cover of $U$ 
725	in the fpqc topology, then show that the following 
726	sequence of sets is exact:
727	$$\Quot_{E/X/S}(U) \to \prod_i \Quot_{E/X/S}(U_i)
728	\stackrel{\to}{\scriptstyle\to} \prod_{i,j}\Quot_{E/X/S}(U_i\times_UU_j)$$
729	
730	
731	
732	
733	\medskip
734	
735	\textbf{(7) } \textbf{Valuative criterion for properness }
736	When $X\to S$ is proper, show that the morphism of functors
737	$\Quot_{E/X/S} \to h_S$ satisfies the valuative criterion of properness
738	with respect to discrete valuation rings, that is, 
739	if $R$ is a discrete valuation ring together
740	with a given morphism $\spec R \to S$ making it an $S$-scheme, 
741	show that the restriction map 
742	$\Quot_{E/X/S}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}(\spec K)$
743	is bijective, where $K$ is the quotient field of $R$ 
744	and $\spec K$ is regarded as an $S$-scheme in the obvious way.
745	
746	
747	
748	\medskip
749	
750	\textbf{(8) } \textbf{Counterexample of Hironaka }
751	Hironaka constructed a $3$-dimensional smooth proper scheme $X$ over 
752	complex numbers $\C$, together with a free action of the group
753	$G = \Z/(2)$, for which the quotient $X/G$ does not exist as a scheme.
754	(See Example 3.4.1 in Hartshorne [H] Appendix B for construction of
755	$X$. We leave the definition of the $G$ action and the proof that
756	$X/G$ does not exist to the reader.)
757	In particular, this means the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{X/\C}$
758	is not representable by a scheme.

771	%\newpage
772	
773	\centerline{\large\bf Construction of Grassmannian}
774	
775	
776	\medskip
777	
778	The following explicit construction of 
779	the Grassmannian scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$ is best understood 
780	as the construction of a quotient
781	$GL_{d,\Z}\backslash V$, where $V$ is the scheme of all
782	$d\times r$ matrices of rank $d$, and the group-scheme
783	$GL_{d,\Z}$ acts on $V$ on the left by matrix multiplication. However,
784	we will not use the language of group-scheme actions 
785	here, instead, we give a direct elementary construction of 
786	the Grassmannian scheme.
787	
788	The reader can take $d=1$ in what follows, in a first reading, to
789	get the special case $\grass(r,1) = \PP^{r-1}_{\Z}$, which 
790	has another construction as $\proj \Z[x_1,\ldots,x_r]$. 
791	
792	
793	
794	\medskip
795	
796	
797	
798	\textbf{Construction by gluing together affine patches }
799	For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, the Grassmannian scheme
800	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological 
801	quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
802	$\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$, 
803	can be explicitly constructed as follows.
804	
805	
806	
807	If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and 
808	$I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal 
809	to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will 
810	mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
811	$I$. 
812	
813	
814	For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$, 
815	consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
816	$X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
817	entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$. 
818	Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
819	$x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically 
820	isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$. 
821	
822	For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let 
823	$P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
824	where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
825	Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
826	the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
827	the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
828	on $U^I_J$. 
829	
830	For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism 
831	$\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
832	defined as follows. The images of the variables 
833	$x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
834	$\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
835	In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
836	so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$.
837	
838	Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and 
839	we leave it to the reader to verify that 
840	for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of 
841	cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition 
842	$\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
843	is satisfied. Therefore the schemes 
844	$U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the 
845	${r\choose d}$ different 
846	subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
847	can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
848	finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
849	isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
850	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$.
851	
852	
853	
854	\medskip
855	
856	\textbf{Separatedness }
857	The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
858	can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ 
859	defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, 
860	and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme.
861	 
862	
863	\medskip
864	
865	\textbf{Properness }
866	We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
867	verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
868	Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
869	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by 
870	a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
871	one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
872	where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes 
873	the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
874	$\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
875	$f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
876	lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
877	
878	Now consider the homomorphism
879	$g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
880	$$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
881	Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
882	and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
883	$\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. 
884	As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
885	$\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$. 
886	Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
887	factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
888	schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
889	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
890	separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that 
891	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. 
892	
893	
894	\medskip
895	
896	
897	
898	\textbf{Universal quotient }
899	We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
900	together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
901	On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism 
902	$u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
903	by the matrix $X^I$. Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
904	for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
905	$(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
906	by putting 
907	$$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$
908	This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$, 
909	so we get a surjective homomorphism 
910	$u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
911	
912	
913	\medskip
914	\textbf{Projective embedding }
915	As $\U$ is given by the transition functions $g_{I,J}$ described above, 
916	the determinant line bundle 
917	$\det(\U)$ is given by the transition functions
918	$\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)$.
919	For each $I$, we define a global section 
920	$$\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\grass(r,d),\det(\U))$$ 
921	by putting $\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I \in 
922	\Gamma(U^J,{\mathcal O}_{U^J})$ in terms of the trivialization over the open cover
923	$(U^J)$. 
924	We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections $\sigma_I$ form
925	a linear system which is base point free and separates points 
926	relative to $\spec \Z$, and so gives an embedding of 
927	$\grass(r,d)$ into $\PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m = {r\choose d}-1$.
928	This is a closed embedding by the properness of 
929	$\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$. In particular, $\det(\U)$
930	is a relatively very ample line bundle on $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$.
931	
932	
933	\medskip
934	
935	{\footnotesize
936	\textbf{Note } The $\sigma_I$ are known as the Pl\"ucker coordinates, 
937	and these satisfy certain quadratic polynomials known as the Pl\"ucker 
938	relations, which define the projective image of the Grassmannian.
939	We will not need these facts. }%end of footnotesize 
940

948	
949	\section{Castelnuovo-Mumford Regularity}
950	
951	
952	
953	Mumford's deployment of $m$-regularity led to a simplification in the 
954	construction of Quot schemes. The original construction of Grothendieck 
955	had instead relied on Chow coordinates. 
956	
957	
958	
959	
960	Let $k$ be a field and let $\F$ be a coherent sheaf on the 
961	projective space $\PP^n$ over $k$. Let $m$ be an integer.
962	The sheaf $\F$ is said to be \textbf{$m$-regular} if we have
963	$$H^i(\PP^n, \F(m-i)) =0\mbox{ for each }i \ge 1.$$
964	The definition, which may look strange at first sight, is suitable
965	for making inductive arguments on $n=\dim(\PP^n)$
966	by restriction to a suitable hyperplane. If $H\subset \PP^n$ is
967	a hyperplane which does not contain any associated point of $\F$,
968	then we have a short exact sheaf sequence
969	$$0 \to \F(m-i-1) \stackrel{\alpha}{\to} \F(m-i) \to \F_H(m-i)\to 0$$
970	where the map $\alpha$ is locally given by multiplication with
971	a defining equation of $H$, hence is injective.
972	The resulting long exact cohomology sequence
973	$$\ldots \to H^i(\PP^n, \F(m-i)) \to H^i(\PP^n, \F_H(m-i)) 
974	\to H^{i+1}(\PP^n, \F(m-i-1)) \to \ldots$$
975	shows that if $\F$ is $m$-regular, then so is its restriction $\F_H$
976	(with the \textit{same value\,} for $m$) 
977	to a hyperplane $H\simeq \PP^{n-1}$ which does not contain any 
978	associated point of $\F$.
979	Note that whenever $\F$ is coherent, the set of associated points of
980	$\F$ is finite, so there will exist at least one such hyperplane $H$
981	when the field $k$ is infinite.
982	
983	The following lemma is due to Castelnuovo, according to 
984	Mumford's {\sl Curves on a surface}.
985	
986	\begin{lemma}\label{Castelnuovo}

6:The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of $C$ in the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine $g$-dimensional abelian variety even when $C(k) = \emptyset$ (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$ and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus $0$ the witness object is instead the trivial scheme $\Spec k$, \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma $+$ additivity Corollary~1.5 $+$ Corollary~1.2 $+$ rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-$\bar k$ declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries $[\mathtt{CharZero}]$, is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-$0$ witness consumes.
10:For a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$, the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from $C$. Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
16:  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
19:is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing $P_0$ gives a translation isomorphism $\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}$, and the Stein factorisation of $\sigma$ yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.

173:  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of $C$ equals the genus $g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to $\Jac(C)$ at the identity is canonically identified with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of $\mathcal O_C$ is classified by an element of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$, and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$. Smoothness of $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any $k$-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of $\Jac(C)$ at every $k$-rational point is the same as at the identity.

198:  Mathematically, properness of the Albanese variety is part of its construction. In the Picard-scheme route, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, which is itself proper over $\Spec k$ because $C$ is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for $\Pic$ of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ (and hence $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ after translation) follows from properness of $\Sym^g(C)$ together with the universal closedness of the surjection $\sigma$. In either case, properness descends to $\Jac(C)$.

220:  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Lem.~(lem:agps), Prp.~(prp:pic0).}
228:  Mathematically, the Albanese variety $\Jac(C)$ is the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$ (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, $\Jac(C)$ arises as the Stein factorisation of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ followed by a translation; $\Sym^g(C)$ is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of $C^g$, itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.
241:The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: $\Jac(C)$ is the connected component of the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. An equivalent route, available whenever a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ and translate by $-gP$.

1694	\section{Relative effective divisors}\label{sc:red}
1695	
1696	Grothendieck constructed the Picard scheme by taking a suitable family
1697	of effective divisors and forming the quotient modulo linear
1698	equivalence.  This section develops the basic theory of these notions.
1699	
1700	\begin{sbs}[Effective divisors] \label{sb:ediv}
1701	 A closed subscheme $D\subset X$ is called an {\it effective (Cartier)
1702	divisor\/} if its ideal $\mc I$ is invertible.  Given an $\mc
1703	O_{\!X}$-module $\mc F$ and   $n\in\bb Z$, set
1704		$$\mc F(nD):=\mc F\ox\mc I^{\ox -n}.$$
1705	
1706	In particular, $\mc O_{\!X}(-D)=\mc I$.  So the inclusion $\mc I\into\mc
1707	O_{\!X}$ yields, via tensor product with $\mc O_{\!X}(D)$, an injection
1708	$\mc O_{\!X}\into \mc O_{\!X}(D)$, which, in turn, corresponds to a
1709	global section of $\mc O_{\!X}(D)$.  This section is not arbitrary since
1710	it corresponds to an injection.  Sections corresponding to injections
1711	are termed {\it regular}.
1712	
1713	Conversely, given an arbitrary invertible sheaf $\mc L$ on $X$, let
1714	$\uH^0(X,\mc L)_{\reg}$ denote the subset of $\uH^0(X,\mc L)$ consisting
1715	of the regular sections, those corresponding to injections $\mc
1716	L^{-1}\into\mc O_{\!X}$.  And let $|\mc L|$ denote the set of effective
1717	divisors $D$ such that $\mc O_{\!X}(D)$ is, in some way, isomorphic to $\mc
1718	L$.  For historical reasons, $|\mc L|$ is called the {\it complete
1719	linear system\/} associated to $\mc L$ (but $|\mc L|$ needn't be a $\I P^n$ 
1720	if $X$ isn't integral).
1721	 \end{sbs}
1722	
1723	\begin{ex}\label{ex:lsys}
1724	 Under the conditions of (\ref{sb:ediv}), establish a canonical isomorphism
1725	     $$\uH^0(X,\mc L)_{\reg}\big/\uH^0(X,\mc O_{\!X}^*)\risom|\mc L|.$$
1726	  \end{ex}
1727	
1728	\begin{dfn}\label{df:red}
1729	 A {\it relative effective divisor\/} on $X/S$ is an effective divisor
1730	$D\subset X$ that is  $S$-flat.
1731	 \end{dfn}
1732	
1733	\begin{lem}\label{lm:ctn}
1734	 Let $D\subset X$ be a closed subscheme, $x\in D$ a point, and $s\in S$
1735	its image.  Then the following statements are equivalent:
1736	 \begin{enumerate}
1737	  \item The subscheme $D\subset X$ is a relative effective divisor at
1738	$x$ (that is, in a neighborhood of $x$).
1739	  \item The schemes $X$ and $D$ are $S$-flat at $x$, and the fiber $D_s$
1740	is an effective divisor on $X_s$ at $x$. 
1741	  \item The scheme $X$ is $S$-flat at $x$, and the subscheme $D\subset
1742	X$ is cut out at $x$ by one element that is regular (a nonzerodivisor)
1743	on the fiber $X_s$.
1744	 \end{enumerate}
1745	\end{lem}
1746	
1747	\begin{proof} 
1748	 For convenience, set $A:=\mc O_{S,s}$ and denote its residue field by
1749	$k$.  In addition, set $B:=\mc O_{\!X,x}$ and $C:=\mc O_{D,x}$.  Then
1750	$B\ox_A k=\mc O_{\!X_s,x}$.
1751	
1752	Assume (i), and let's prove (ii).  By hypothesis, $D$ is an effective
1753	divisor at $x$.  So there is a regular element $b\in B$ that generates
1754	the ideal of $D$.  Multiplication by $b$ defines a short exact sequence
1755		$$0\to B\to B\to C\to0.$$
1756	 In turn, this sequence induces the following exact sequence:
1757	 $$\Tor^A_1(B,k)\to\Tor^A_1(B,k)\to\Tor^A_1(C,k)\to B\ox k\to B\ox k.$$
1758	
1759	By hypothesis, $D$ is $S$-flat at $x$; hence, $\Tor^A_1(C,k)=0$.  So
1760	$B\ox k\to B\ox k$ is injective.  Its image is the ideal of $D_s$.  Thus
1761	$D_s$ is an effective divisor.
1762	
1763	Since $\Tor^A_1(C,k)=0$, the map $\Tor^A_1(B,k)\to\Tor^A_1(B,k)$ is
1764	surjective.  This map is given by multiplication by $b$, and $b$ lies in
1765	the maximal ideal of $B$.  Also, $\Tor^A_1(B,k)$ is a finitely
1766	generated $B$-module.  Hence,  $\Tor^A_1(B,k)=0$ by Nakayama's lemma.
1767	Therefore, by the local criterion of flatness \cite[Thm.~5.6,
1768	p.~98]{SGA1} or \cite[Thm.~6.1, p.~73]{OB72}, $B$ is $A$-flat; in other
1769	words, $X$ is $S$-flat at $x$.  Thus (ii) holds.
1770	
1771	Assume (ii).  To prove (iii), denote the ideal of $D$ in $B$ by $I$, and
1772	that of $D_s$ in $B\ox k$ by $I'$.  Take an element $b\in I$ whose image $b'$ 
1773	in $B\ox k$ generates $I'$; such a $b$ exists because $D_s$ is an
1774	effective divisor at $x$ by hypothesis.  For the same reason, $b'$ is
1775	regular.  It remains to prove $b$ generates $I$. 
1776	
1777	Consider the short exact sequence
1778	%% \begin{equation}\label{eq:3a}
1779		$$0\to I\to B\to C\to0.$$
1780	%%  \end{equation}
1781	 By hypothesis, $C$ is $A$-flat.  Hence the map $I\ox k\to B\ox k$ is
1782	injective.  Its image is $I'$, which is generated by $b'$.  So the image
1783	of $b$ in $I\ox k$ generates it.  Hence, by Nakayama's lemma, $b$
1784	generates $I$.  Thus (iii) holds.
1785	 
1786	Assume (iii).  To prove (i), again denote the ideal of $D$ in $B$ by
1787	$I$.  By hypothesis, $I$ is generated by an element $b$ whose image $b'$
1788	in $B\ox k$ is regular.  We have to prove $b$ is regular and $C$ is
1789	$A$-flat.
1790	
1791	The exact sequence $0\to I\to B\to C\to0$ yields this one:
1792	 \begin{equation}\label{eq:3a}
1793	  \Tor^A_1(B,k)\to\Tor^A_1(C,k)\to I\ox k\to B\ox k.
1794	  \end{equation}
1795	 The last map is injective for the following reason.  Since $I=Bb$,
1796	multiplication by $b$ induces a surjection $B\to I$, so a surjection
1797	$B\ox k\to I\ox k$.  Consider the composition
1798		$$B\ox k\to I\ox k\to B\ox k.$$
1799	 It is given by multiplication by $b'$, so is injective because $b'$ is
1800	regular. Hence $B\ox k\risom I\ox k$.  Therefore, $I\ox k\to B\ox k$ is
1801	injective.
1802	
1803	By hypothesis, $B$ is $A$-flat.  So $\Tor^A_1(B,k)=0.$ Hence the
1804	exactness of (\ref{eq:3a}) implies $\Tor^A_1(C,k)=0$.  Therefore, by the
1805	local criterion, $C$ is $A$-flat.
1806	
1807	Since $B$ and $C$ are $A$-flat and $0\to I\to B\to C\to0$ is exact, also
1808	$I$ is $A$-flat.
1809	
1810	Define $K$ by the exact sequence  $0\to K\to B\to I\to0$.  Then the
1811	sequence
1812		$$0\to K\ox k\to B\ox k\to I\ox k\to0$$
1813	 is exact since $I$ is $A$-flat.  But $B\ox k\risom I\ox k$. Hence $K\ox
1814	k=0$.  Therefore, $K=0$ by Nakayama's lemma.  But $K$ is the kernel of
1815	multiplication by $b$ on $B$.  So $b$ is regular.  Thus (i) holds.
1816	 \end{proof}

1818	\begin{ex}\label{ex:sum}
1819	 Let $D$ and $E$ be relative effective divisors on $X/S$, and $D+E$
1820	their sum. Show $D+E$ is a relative effective divisor too.
1821	 \end{ex}
1822	
1823	\begin{dfn}\label{df:div}
1824	 Define a functor $\Div_{X/S}$ by the formula
1825	    $$\Div_{X/S}(T):=\{\,\text{relative effective divisors $D$
1826	     on $X_T/T$}\,\}.$$
1827	 \end{dfn}
1828	
1829	Note $\Div_{X/S}$ is indeed a functor.  Namely, given a relative
1830	effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ and an arbitrary $S$-map $p\:T'\to T$,
1831	we have to see the $T'$-flat closed subscheme $D_{T'}\subset
1832	X_{T'}$ is an effective divisor.  So let $\mc I$ denote the ideal of
1833	$D$.  Since $D$ is $T$-flat, $p_{X_T}^*\mc I$ is equal to the ideal of
1834	$D_{T'}$.  But, since $\mc I$ is invertible, so is $p_{X_T}^*\mc I$.
1835	Thus $D_{T'}$ is a (relative) effective divisor.
1836	
1837	\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
1838	 Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat.  Then $\Div_{X/S}$ is
1839	representable by an open subscheme $\IDiv_{X/S}$ of the Hilbert scheme
1840	$\IHilb_{X/S}$.
1841	 \end{thm}
1842	
1843	\begin{proof}
1844	 Set $H:=\IHilb_{X/S}$, and let $W\subset X\x H$ be the universal
1845	(closed) subscheme, and $q\:W\to H$ the projection.  Let $V$ denote the
1846	set of points $w\in W$ at which $W$ is an effective divisor. Plainly $V$
1847	is open in $W$.  Set $Z:=q(W-V)$.  Then $Z$ is closed because $q$ is
1848	proper.  Set $U:=H-Z$.  Then $U$ is open, and $q^{-1}U$ is an effective
1849	divisor in $X\x U$.  In fact, since $q$ is flat, $q^{-1}U$ is a relative
1850	effective divisor in $X\x U/U$.
1851	
1852	It remains to show that $U$ represents $\Div_{X/S}$.  So let $T$ be an
1853	$S$-scheme, and $D\subset X_T/T$ a relative effective divisor.  By the
1854	universal property of the pair $(H,W)$, there exists a unique map
1855	$g\:T\to H$ such that $g_X^{-1}W=D$.  We have to show that $g$ factors
1856	through $U$.
1857	
1858	For each $t\in T$, the fiber $D_t$ is an effective divisor since it is
1859	obtained by base change (or owing to Lemma~\ref{lm:ctn}).  But
1860	$D_t=W_{g(t)}\ox k_t$ where $k_t$ is the residue field of $t$.  So
1861	$W_{g(t)}$ too is a divisor, as a field extension is faithfully flat.
1862	Hence, since $X\x H$ and $W$ are $H$-flat, $W$ is, by
1863	Lemma~\ref{lm:ctn}, a relative effective divisor along the fiber over
1864	$g(t)$.  Therefore, $g(t)\in U$.  So, since $U$ is open, $g$ factors
1865	through $U$.
1866	 \end{proof}
1867	
1868	\begin{ex}\label{ex:DivC}
1869	  Assume $f\:X\to S$ is flat and is projective Zariski locally over $S$.
1870	Assume its fibers are curves, that is, of {\it pure} dimension 1.  Given
1871	$m\ge1$, let $\Div_{X/S}^m$ be the functor whose $T$-points are the
1872	relative effective divisors $D$ on $X_T/T$ with fibers $D_t$ of degree
1873	$m$.
1874	
1875	Show the $\Div_{X/S}^m$ are representable by open and closed subschemes
1876	of finite type $\IDiv_{X/S}^m\subset\IDiv_{X/S}$, which are disjoint and
1877	cover.

1880	$X_0=\IDiv_{X/S}^1$.

1882	Let  $X_0^m$ be the $m$-fold  $S$-fibered product.  Show there is a natural
1883	$S$-map $$\alpha\:X_0^m\to\IDiv_{X/S}^m,$$ which is given on  $T$-points
1884	by $\alpha(\Gamma_1,\dotsc,\Gamma_m)= \sum \Gamma_i$.

1887	\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:symprod}
1888	 Consider the map $\alpha$ of Exercise~\ref{ex:DivC}.  Plainly $\alpha$
1889	is compatible with permuting the factors of $X_0^m$.  Hence $\alpha$
1890	factors through the symmetric product $X_0^{(m)}$.  In fact, $\alpha$
1891	induces an isomorphism
1892		$$X_0^{(m)}\risom \IDiv_{X_0/S}^m.$$
1893	 This isomorphism is treated in detail in \cite[Prp.~6.3.9,
1894	p.~437]{De73} and in outline in \cite[Prp.~3, p.~254]{BLR}.
1895	 \end{rmk}
1896	
1897	\begin{sbs}[The module $\mc Q$]\label{sb:Q}
1898	 Assume $f\:X\to S$ is proper, and let $\mc F$ be a coherent $\mc
1899	O_{\!X}$-module flat over $S$.  Recall from \cite[7.7.6]{EGAIII2} that there
1900	exist a coherent $\mc O_{\!S}$-module $\mc Q$ and an isomorphism of functors
1901	in the quasi-coherent $\mc O_{\!S}$-module $\mc N$:
1902	 \begin{equation}\label{eq:Q}
1903		q\:\SHom(\mc Q, \mc N)\risom f_*(\mc F\ox f^*\mc N).
1904	 \end{equation}
1905	  The pair $(\mc Q, q)$ is unique, up to unique isomorphism, and by
1906	\cite[7.7.9]{EGAIII2}, forming it commutes with changing the base, in
1907	particular, with localizing.
1908	
1909	Fix $s\in S$ and assume $S=\Spec(\mc O_{S,s})$.  Note that the following
1910	conditions are equivalent:
1911	 \begin{enumerate}
1912	  \item the $\mc O_{\!S}$-module $\mc Q$ is free (or equivalently,
1913	projective);
1914	  \item the functor $\mc N\mapsto f_*(\mc F\ox f^*\mc N)$ is right
1915	exact;
1916	  \item for all $\mc N$, the natural map is an isomorphism, $f_*(\mc
1917	F)\ox \mc N\risom f_*(\mc F\ox f^*\mc N)$;
1918	  \item the natural map is a surjection,
1919	 $\uH^0(X,\mc F)\ox k_s\onto\uH^0(X_s,\mc F_s)$.
1920	 \end{enumerate}
1921	 Indeed, the equivalence of (i)--(iii) is elementary and straightforward.
1922	Moreover, (iv) is a special case of (iii).  Conversely, (iv) implies
1923	(iii) by \cite[7.7.10]{EGAIII2} or \cite[Cor.~5.1 p.~72]{OB72}; this
1924	useful implication is known as the ``property of exchange.''
1925	
1926	In addition, (i)--(iv) are implied by the following condition:
1927	 \begin{enumerate}\setcounter{enumi}{4}
1928	  \item the first cohomology group of the fiber vanishes, $\uH^1(X_s,\mc
1929	F_s)=0$.
1930	 \end{enumerate} 
1931	 Indeed, (v) implies that $\tu R^1f_*(\mc F\ox f^*\mc N)=0$ for all $\mc
1932	N$ by \cite[7.5.3]{EGAIII2} or \cite[Cor.~2.1 p.~68]{OB72}; in turn, this
1933	vanishing implies (ii) owing to the long exact sequence of higher direct
1934	images.
1935	 \end{sbs}

1937	\begin{ex}\label{ex:gc&r}
1938	%%   Assume $f\:X\to S$ is proper and flat.  Assume its geometric
1939	  Assume $f\:X\to S$ is proper and flat, and its geometric fibers are
1940	reduced and connected.  Show $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ holds
1941	universally.
1942	 \end{ex}
1943	
1944	\begin{dfn}\label{df:LinSys}
1945	Let $\mc L$ be an invertible sheaf on $X$.  Define a subfunctor
1946	$\LinSys_{\mc L/X/S}$ of $\Div_{X/S}$ by the formula
1947	  \begin{multline*}
1948	   \LinSys_{\mc L/X/S}(T):=\{\,\text{relative effective divisors $D$
1949	     on $X_T/T$ such that}\\
1950	     \text{$\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\simeq \mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc N$
1951	%%      \text{$\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\simeq(\mc L|X_T)\ox f_T^*\mc N$
1952			for some invertible sheaf $\mc N$ on }T\,\}.
1953	  \end{multline*}
1954	 \end{dfn}
1955	
1956	Notice the similarity of this definition with that,
1957	Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}, of $\Pic_{X/S}$: both definitions work with
1958	isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves on $X_T$ modulo $\Pic(T)$, in
1959	the hope of producing a representable functor.  Here, this hope is
1960	fulfilled under suitable hypotheses on $f$, according to the next
1961	theorem.
1962	
1963	\begin{thm}\label{th:LinSys} Assume $X/S$ is proper and flat, and its
1964	geometric fibers are integral.  Let $\mc L$ be an invertible sheaf on
1965	$X$, and let $\mc Q$ be the $\mc O_{\!S}$-module associated in
1966	Subsection~\ref{sb:Q} to $\mc F:=\mc L$. Set $L:=\I P(\mc Q)$.  Then $L$
1967	represents $\LinSys_{\mc L/X/S}$.
1968	 \end{thm}
1969	
1970	\begin{proof}
1971	 Let $D\in\LinSys_{\mc L/X/S}(T)$.  Say $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\simeq \mc
1972	L_T\ox f_T^*\mc N$.  Then $\mc N$ is determined up to isomorphism.
1973	Indeed, let $\mc N'$ be a second choice.  Then $$\mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc
1974	N\simeq\mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc N'.$$  So $f_T^*\mc N\simeq f_T^*\mc N'$
1975	since $\mc L$ is invertible.  Hence $\mc N\simeq\mc N'$ by
1976	Lemma~\ref{lm:fff}, which applies to $f_T\:X_T\to T$ since $\mc
1977	O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ holds universally by Exercise~\ref{ex:gc&r}.
1978	
1979	Say $D$ is defined by $\sigma\in\uH^0(X_T,\mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc N)$.
1980	Now, forming $\mc Q$ commutes with changing the base to $T$, and so
1981	(\ref{eq:Q}) becomes
1982	 \begin{equation}\label{eq:QT}
1983	  \SHom(\mc Q_T, \mc N)\risom f_{\smash{T}*}(\mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc N).
1984	 \end{equation}
1985	 Hence $\sigma$ corresponds to a map $u\:\mc Q_T\to\mc N$.
1986	 
1987	Let $t\in T$.  Since $D$ is a relative effective divisor on $X_T/T$, its
1988	fiber $D_t$ is a divisor on $X_t$ by Lemma~\ref{lm:ctn}.  Since $D_t$ is
1989	defined by $\sigma_t\in \uH^0(X_t,\mc L|X_t)$, necessarily $\sigma_t
1990	\neq 0$.  But $\sigma_t$ corresponds to $u\ox k_t$, so $u\ox k_t\neq 0$.
1991	Now, $\mc N$ is invertible, so $\mc N\ox k_t$ is a $k_t$-vector space of
1992	dimension 1.  So $u\ox k_t$ is surjective.  Hence, by Nakayama's lemma,
1993	$u$ is surjective at $t$.  But $t$ is arbitrary.  So $u$ is surjective
1994	everywhere.
1995	
1996	Therefore, $u\:\mc Q_T\to\mc N$ defines an $S$-map $p\:T\to L$ by
1997	\cite[4.2.3]{EGAII}.  Since $(\mc N, u)$ is determined up to
1998	isomorphism, a second choice yields the same $p$.
1999	
2000	Plainly, this construction is functorial in $T$.  Thus we obtain a map
2001	of functors,
2002	\begin{equation*}
2003	 	\Lambda\:\LinSys_{\mc L/X/S}(T)\to L(T).
2004	 \end{equation*}
2005	 Let us prove  $\Lambda$  is an isomorphism.
2006	
2007	Let $p\in L(T)$, so  $p\:T\to L$ is an $S$-map.  Then $p$  arises from a
2008	%% Consider an $S$-map $p\:T\to L$.  It arises from a
2009	surjection $u\:\mc Q_T\onto\mc N$; namely, $u=p^*\alpha$ where
2010	$\alpha\:\mc Q_L \onto\mc O(1)$ is the tautological map.  Moreover,
2011	there is only one such pair $(\mc N, u)$ up to isomorphism.
2012	
2013	Via the isomorphism in (\ref{eq:QT}), the surjection $u$
2014	corresponds to a global section $\sigma\in\uH^0(X_T,\mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc
2015	N)$. Let $t\in T$.  Then $u\ox k_t$ is surjective, so $u\ox k_t\neq 0$.
2016	But $u\ox k_t$ corresponds to $\sigma_t\in \uH^0(X_t,\mc L|X_t)$, so
2017	$\sigma_t \neq 0$.  But $X_t$ is integral since the geometric fibers of
2018	$X/S$ are integral by hypothesis.  Hence $\sigma_t$ is regular.
2019	
2020	The section $\sigma$ defines a map $(\mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc N)^{-1} \to \mc
2021	O_{\!X_T}$.  Its image is the ideal of a closed subscheme $D\subset X$,
2022	which is cut out locally by one element; moreover, on the fiber $X_t$,
2023	this element corresponds to $\sigma_t$, so is regular.  Hence $D$ is a
2024	relative effective divisor on $X_T/T$ by Lemma~\ref{lm:ctn}.  In fact,
2025	$D\in \LinSys_{\mc L/X/S}(T)$.  Plainly, $D$ is the only such divisor
2026	corresponding to $(\mc N, u)$, so mapping to $p$ under $\Lambda$.
2027	
2028	Thus $\Lambda$ is an isomorphism.  In other words, $L$ represents
2029	$\LinSys_{\mc L/X/S}$.
2030	 \end{proof}

2034	natural relative effective divisor $W$ on $X_L\big/L$ such that
2035	 $$\mc O_{X_L}(W)=\mc L_L\ox f_L^*\mc O_L(1).$$
2036	 Furthermore, $W$ possesses the following universal property: given any
2037	$S$-scheme $T$ and any relative effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ such
2038	that $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\simeq\mc L_T\ox f_T^*\mc N$ for some
2039	invertible sheaf $\mc N$ on $T$, there exist a unique $S$-map $w\:T\to
2040	L$ such that $(1\x w)^{-1}W=D$.
